title: Open sourcing the superchain (with Optimism)

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: I think there's been a lot of apt community criticism. And I think the biggest thing that we've learned is no matter how much time you spend debating strategic hypotheses, nothing beats putting code into production in front of users. It instantly validates or invalidates a whole slew of hypotheses. And so just pick the thing that's quickest to ship, ship it, and then see what happens.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Hello and welcome to Web3 with A16Z, a show about building the next era of the Internet by the team at A16Z Crypto. That includes me, host Robert Hackett. Today's episode features Jing Wang, CEO and Executive Director of the Optimism Foundation, along with A16Z Crypto CTO Eddie Lazarin. We discuss the peculiarities of open source software, including the incentives that bind contributors together, tradeoffs, between the freedom to customize versus sticking to standards, and the challenges in setting up and running a foundation. We also cover the nuances of governance and accountability, the importance of vibes, the indispensability of shipping products versus debating roadmaps, and the vision behind the so-called superchain. As head of the Optimism Foundation, Wang helped steward the Optimism Collective, a band of decentralized companies, communities, contributors, and others who are using a suite of open source software called the OP Stack to scale the Ethereum blockchain network. The OP Stack also powers a number of popular Layer 2 rollups, including Base, which we covered in last week's episode with its creator and lead, Coinbase's head of protocols, Jesse Pollack. Be sure also to check out the A16Z YouTube channel for video podcast episodes, as well as talks from our recent startup accelerator program, CSX, featuring Jing, co-founder Carl Florian, and A16Z. As a reminder, none of the following should be taken as tax, business, legal, or investment advice. See a16zcrypto.com slash disclosures for more important information, including a link to a list of our investments. Jing, tell me about the early days of Ethereum. I'm sure you've got some stories from back in the day. You've been a part of this community since it basically was a community.

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: I first joined as a Bitcoiner, and I didn't come into Ethereum. Until 2017, when basically the first early, more mainstream wave of people entered also. I would say that I was attracted by the culture. You really felt like you were one of many building towards something together, which is something that we've really tried to replicate or follow with optimism culture. And it wasn't about what you individually or independently could accomplish. So those were early Ethereum vibes. I think Ethereum and L1 Governance has done a really great job of keeping those vibes. And something that I've been talking about a lot with my team internally is, you know, what aspects of that culture are actually scalable? And what aspects of it are difficult to scale? Because when you have like widespread cooperative culture, you really only need like one bad apple to start trying to take credit for themselves or self-promote to make it no longer, rational strategy for participation to be cooperative for everybody else who's contributing together.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: I think it's interesting that, Jing, you came at this as a Bitcoiner. I met Vitalik back in 2016. And I remember him just talking about the Bitcoin maxis and how, you know, he was just so opposed to the Bitcoin maxi worldview. So how does one attract someone from a different tribe over to their tribe? You know, what has to be done? What about the culture do you have to find compelling to actually get someone to make that switch?

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: That's a tough one. That's a tough one. What a difficult question.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: It is a tough one, yeah. About the Ethereum culture?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Well, I guess I'd be interested to know what you found compelling about Ethereum culture and then also whether those lessons are generalizable to other projects.

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: I mean, like flexibility and innovation. I think it's a design of Bitcoin that changes are difficult to make. And as much as possible, you want ossification. Ossification is easy to, you know, you want to have a plan around. It makes Bitcoin super reliable for a lot of different use cases. And for me personally, that's just, you know, I wanted to spend my time feeling like I had an impact in how a piece of technology was developing. And I think Ethereum is a lot more flexible, a lot more extensible. There were grants being put forth to cultivating this kind of activity. It was something that was encouraged and rewarded. People give talks at conferences. And. And I think the time that I spent working on Ethereum was more likely to equate to something that was broadly impactful for a lot of people in the community, whereas I just didn't see that path for myself with my skill set in the Bitcoin community. And I don't think there's anything wrong with that. I think different cultures exist for a reason. And, you know, never in a million years would I want Bitcoin to be like Ethereum, but never in a million years would I want Ethereum to go the other direction either.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah. How does one go about building on something that ultimately you kind of want to recede into the background, right? Like a lot of this blockchain tech, the chain stuff, we sort of want to abstract it away so that people don't even realize that they're using it. You know, they're just sort of getting these benefits without thinking deeply about like the exact nature of the technology behind it. So that presents like a little bit of a marketing challenge, right? Like getting people to be excited about a technology and yet at the same time kind of wanting that technology to recede into the background.

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: So the people who want to contribute are not the same people as those who want the technology to recede into the background. It's like nerds, protocol nerds versus application developers. For application developers, they just want to focus on bringing their product to market, accessing users and distribution. And they don't want to suffer the UX of your infrastructure. Look at the onboarding flow, however many steps it is, that's what users will have to inherit. And so for those people, they definitely want it to recede into the background. But there are a lot of folks like Sunnyside Labs, Wonderland, Base that want to contribute to the infrastructure, either because they need it to improve for their core product requirements or because they got into crypto super early as protocol nerds. Right. And they want to contribute their efforts to a credibly open sourced standard.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah. You got me thinking all these things, Robert, and shame what you're saying, because we have basically from the beginning of crypto with a few exceptions. There are definitely a few exceptions. So much of it is dev stuff and like builder stuff. And the target demo is kind of technical people. That's just been crypto for so long. We don't need all of humanity thinking about this. But it's true. Like they really like that. So eventually I hope it does withdraw or fade into the background. In some sense. But, you know, I mean, maybe it's kind of like maybe one way. Another analogy is like cars, you know, like most people for most people, cars are just like a way to get around whatever you just get around. Like I get into the thing, I pay for it, whatever. But then there's still a community of car nuts and there will always be a community of car nuts. My father is one of them. Yeah. And like they love the details and they're just always going on about it. And it's really neat. And those are the people you want to cater to with certain products. Obviously, those, you know, those products are not applicable for everybody. And so the point is to say is that at the stage we are in crypto, we're still like the car net phase because it's early and everybody has to be an expert and do all those things. So I don't see that going away for the foreseeable future. Like just for, you know, it's going to be years more of that probably at the same time that there are great mainstream applications. It's too important.

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: Well, it's like the same flavor of nut. As like Satoshi, Vitalik, you know, it's like those nuts that attracted all the early nuts. And then the early nuts had a social hierarchy of like nuttiness. And it was like protocol engineering, cryptography, mechanism design that like brought you to the peak of that ladder. And I think that I think that's true today. Also, people think that, you know, like protocol engineering is like cool and sexy and want to make changes or modifications at the protocol. Layer that they don't necessarily need to be making because it feels cool to do so.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Totally agree.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Let's back way up. Maybe I could ask quite broadly, why should anybody do stuff on chain? Like what's the benefit? Why should people be transacting and whatever on chain?

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: I think something that is underappreciated about building in Web3 outside of the like decentralization meme is just straight up the open source culture. And this is achievable with or without decentralization. But I think what we have in Web3 is a lot of standards that people have organized around. And having these standards makes building tools easier. It makes building applications easier. Anything from the ERC-20 standard to bridge standards, the EVM as a standard, solidity. It just brings when we talk about like composability and interoperability, it's not just about co-locating on the same state space. A lot of it. It's about using the same tools and the same standards.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah, I think that's so apt. I've been having a lot of conversations with people who were doing NFT stuff a couple of years ago. And it's really interesting to see the way they're sort of like contorting themselves. Like they're still using NFTs because the standards are so valuable and there's nothing sort of like it. But, you know, in order to message it to other people, they're like, oh, no, they're digital assets or they're digital collectibles or they're this and that sort of like obscuring the back end. But, you know, when you ask if there's anything else they could be using, it's sort of like, well, not really. You know, it's this is really the only thing that we can use that to accomplish what we need.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, I think, you know, like one common answer is like decentralization. It's power to the people. Like what power, where, to who? Like, are we really accomplishing that? You know, maybe in some pockets and maybe that's a goal, but I don't think it's achieved and I don't think it's the current primary value prop.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah, we have this conversation a lot here. About like, what does decentralization mean? It's such like a big, unwieldy word. It kind of obscures what's going on. And I've always really liked the way Chris Dixon describes it as, you know, community owned and operated networks. I feel like that that kind of makes the point stick.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, it's interesting because it does mean a lot of things and it means something different in every context that you use it. There's like authoritative decentralization. Who has the ability to upgrade the system? Who has the ability to push forward a proposal? There's operational decentralization. Like who has the right to execute the work? Who has the right to administer a certain process? And then there's also technical decentralization. It's like, you know, the number of nodes in the network, the resilience of it, the distribution, either geographic or otherwise. And in each of these aspects, the word decentralization is actually pointing to a slightly different value prop. Maybe it's like capture resistance. Maybe it's redundancy. It's the power is located somewhere else in the network than the obvious center. Right. That's like really what it means. And it could be located in a lot of places. It could be there could be no power located in the center because it's immutable. There could be no power located in the center because it's been explicitly distributed to a certain set of stakeholders. It could be like, you know, you see, like just something's not being in the center does not really explain what it is. That's why it's so easy to argue so long about what we even mean when we talk about decentralization. But the reason people harp on it is because there's so many. Interesting things that you can achieve if you find a technical or social or social technical means of taking the power out of the center. This is a really cool design space that's even linguistically underexplored. Right. It's like it's just we don't have a lot of great words.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah. My shot at the answer, Robert, like the quick like why should anybody do something on chain and do things on chain? I love Jing's answer. Like, you know, open source libraries can monetize. They've never been able to monetize. They've been able never been able. They capture resilient network effects beyond like the use of the code. Putting that on chain.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: The traditional way to monetize open source is sort of like you've got the open source code and then you kind of like build the service on it that's optimized and give all these sort of like nice add ons to businesses. Yeah.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: And then if it's in the wrong place, then they just end up re centralizing it. Whereas if you put it on chain and you allow the network effects to accumulate there, then the open source can live forever, maybe. But another cut that I really like is a lot. Of complicated things require an intermediary. You just need that. And the question is whether you rely on a trusted intermediary or a trustless one. And if you do stuff on chain, you're using a trust minimized intermediary. And that's really, really cool because you can take all these things for granted. So we talk about all the time, you know, like that's the that's the basic idea. You can choose this. You can choose that. They all remain interoperable. Other people can build on those things. And as long as you trust that programming environment, you can trust that. Things behave as you'll expect them to behave.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah. This sort of ambiguity around decentralization and what it means in different ways, you can kind of slice and dice it. It makes me wonder, Jing, what aspect of decentralization is most important to optimism?

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: I think there are a lot of different layers of our stack that require decentralization and all four different things. So for the governance of the protocol and its upgrades, capture resistance is the most important. Important thing. And then for the technical protocol, it's not just capture resistance, but it's also security and resilience of the network. And so all of these value props are achieved by decentralizing in different ways. Maybe it's your token in the hands of a lot of people. Maybe it's setting up a bicameral voting system. Maybe it's by having a multiprover system. And in terms of the operation of optimism, we also think about decentralization as something to aim for. But it's not for the security of the system and it's not for the capture resistance of the protocol. It's just something that doubles down on our open sourciness. Like we're fully MIT licensed. And one way to look at that is, wow, that sucks because everyone can look at your code and fork it and you have no moat. And another way to look at that is, well, it's also way easier to build a standard and attract an ecosystem of contributors outside of what your recruiting team is capable of finding. And. That's an advantage that we have. We've been able to execute vast swaths of the code base through a community and through external contributors. So when you think about actually like, you know, community ownership, who is your community? Who's owning what? And I think these are the different aspects of decentralization that we care about internally.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah. You mentioned a few pieces there from governance, sort of making the governance of the system resilient to capture, to making the technology able to resist attack. And. And then also just sort of like the ethos underlying the organization as well. I'd love to bite off each of those in turn. But maybe we can start with that last one. I wonder, you know, being sort of the head of a foundation that is, you know, working on these sort of open source decentralized projects. How do you sort of like live and breathe that that ethos of decentralization in your in your day to day work?

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: Something I say to my team often is that we're not even aware of the. Decentralization biases that we have. And because we have those biases, it's actually difficult to like propagate consistent decision making. So let's say like you want to be fully MIT licensed and open source. Well, how many companies out there are actually operating this way? How many of us have experience operating that way? The natural tendency is to go and hire a bunch of people to your organization because we live and breathe by the meme that it's easier to execute with centralized planning. But is it is that true? Like, I don't actually think it is. You guys remember how like, you know, before the pandemic, all companies required that you go to the office and show up in person because everyone believed that that was the best way to work because, you know, remote was just not as high context. But the pandemic forced everyone to write things down very clearly, write down decisions, do meetings really well, be more crisp about the strategy, do planning better. And so I actually think that operating. For a remote culture made it a lot easier for companies to scale beyond 20, 100, et cetera, people. And so similarly for, you know, quote unquote, decentralized workforce, I think it's similar to going remote. Like, yeah, you know, conceptually, we're all accustomed to hiring people into our own teams with really clear accountability and an HR team and these structures that we're familiar with. But what if you were forced to write your spec or the. Problem that you're trying to solve so fucking clearly that an external team doesn't have to talk to you and they know exactly what needs to be done to solve that problem. How much more could you scale execution if your team was capable of planning, writing and operating in that way?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: I want to build on that a little bit because there's this thing people may or may not have heard of called the theory of the firm. This is Ronald Coase, the economist. Yeah, this is Coase's essay about the theory of the firm. And this is the simplest possible cut for the theory. The firm is that there are different institutions because internal transaction costs. And when I say transaction costs, I don't mean in the like blockchain setting. I mean, transaction costs like we talk, we make a deal, we agree on a thing, we coordinate, we agree on a spec, we negotiate, we pay each other or even write like all these things are very complicated transactions. The internal transaction costs of a firm are lower than between firms. Right. And that's because you can take certain things for granted. Like. Shared infrastructure, alignment towards certain goals, shared managerial structure. There's all these things you can take for granted. And one way I'm interpreting what you're saying, Jing, is that a lot of our defaults, how we think about work are tuned to taking advantage of the low internal transaction costs. But if you have a wide decentralized ecosystem where you can have a bunch of partners who have slightly different goals but are aligned in some key ways, you may actually have a much wider set, a very, very wide set. Of external contributors. Right. And even if the external transaction costs are a little bit higher, which they are, because like there's all these different people, they're far away. The scale of that is so big and so powerful because of putting things on chain, sharing the code, building on top of each other stuff, composability, all those things. The benefits of very, very high quality coordination with your external contributors is high enough that it's worth enduring those high external transaction costs. And actually optimizing to those in the culture of the company.

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: Exactly. Like, what is external? What outside of payroll? Why is that fundamentally a lower transaction cost? Why can't you just do the onboarding or the culture setting or whatever process for contributions to reduce those transaction costs?

segments.text: Yeah. And HR stuff is hard and important or whatever. But the opportunity we have with everybody coordinating. On the ways to make uniformly valuable block space as like a optimism example. The benefits are so high. We're kind of leaving a lot on the table if we're preoccupied with the internal transaction costs.

segments.text: Exactly. Yeah. I remember us having this like HR conversation around going remote. And, you know, every like six months or so.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Okay. So basically, there are two paths you can take. You can fork the code and do your own thing. You mentioned Blast has done this and not be part of the super chain. Or you can take the code and join the team, join the super group and pay your dues, as it were. Tell me more about these two paths and why someone would want to indeed take the latter route.

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: The level of maturity of the technology within the blockchain industry at large is... A lot less mature than what people would expect from Web2 when they look at the kinds of valuations that exist within the blockchain industry. So all this infrastructure is incredibly novel, new, untested, feature incomplete, optimism included, optimism especially. And so for a lot of projects that are trying to make a decision about what scaling infrastructure to use, they're not just asking themselves, what is the best tech today? They're also asking themselves, what is likely to be the best tech today? And so for a lot of projects, winning tech down the line. And what does winning tech actually mean? Winning tech could mean the technology or the block space that gets the most network effects. So you have the best distribution to liquidity and users. It could mean the technology that is most likely to become the standard. So whatever tooling that you rely on today is also going to evolve as your project evolves. And I think there are a few explicit design choices that we've made that we believe make it more likely that we will be able to do that.

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: And I think that's a really good point. Both in terms of network effects, as well as in terms of standardization. And being fully open sourced is one of them. So for projects that want to be part of defining the standard, like based on 444, for example, the optimism community and the OP stack give you a really clear path towards contributing to the standards that the entire super chain operates on. And so not only do you have a cleaner path to making the modifications, that you want to make, that also makes it more likely that the infrastructure and the code base improves for everybody else that relies on it, which also makes it more likely that this is the stack that gets standardized, aka winning tech by a lot of folks definitions. And so you can go over there in your silo and you can make the modifications to the OP stack that suit you and you don't have to pay anything. But it also means it's that much harder for wallets, block explorers, oracles, bridges to go and implement.

segments.speaker: Jing Wang
segments.text: And I think that's a really good point. Or you can pick to launch a standard config chain, or if the standard config doesn't suit your needs, contribute to the core engineering work required for it to suit your needs, and use a stack that will be supported by all the aforementioned services.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Chris Dixon has a great framework for thinking about this. He says that composability, which is sort of this key property of open source software, composability is to software as compound growth is to finance, or as like, you know, Moore's law is to chip making. title: Token do's and don'ts

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Decentralization isn't some line in a legal book. Decentralization is a technical network property that is amazing, that has unbelievably exciting consequences. That's why the internet is so exciting. The internet is a decentralized system. You can go and build a website and no one can tell you no. And that incredible opportunity means all the brightest minds, entrepreneurs, cypherpunks and crazy hackers and creative people are willing to consider building their future and building the future of technology on a system like that because they can trust it and it's resilient and robust and predictable and powerful and extensible and remixable and recombinable and all the beautiful things that we love about decentralization and permissionless composability. So it's a beautiful coincidence and we're fortunate that the right legal strategy is also the thing that is totally consistent with the crypto ethos from its... beginnings.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Welcome to Web3 with A16Z, a show about building the next generation of the internet. I'm Robert Hackett and today's episode covers all things tokens. That includes what tokens have to do with decentralized protocols, understanding the different types of tokens, and of course, the do's and don'ts of designing and launching a token. Our guests are A16Z Crypto Chief Technology Officer, Eddie Lazarin, as well as A16Z Crypto General Counsel and Head of Decentralization, Miles Jennings, the two of whom have advised many scores of projects on protocol design and token craft. They discuss what sets Web3 apart from earlier technology eras, avoiding common pitfalls in the search for product market fit, how to reason about various designs and strategies, as well as their risk and reward trade-offs, and more. As a reminder, none of the following should be taken for granted. As investment, business, legal, or tax advice. So you can delay the application of securities laws. So this third option has lower legal risk. It has low operational complexity, though, again, as I said, you don't want to be out there marketing. You need to be careful about money transmission, all of these other things. But again, compared to decentralization, it's fairly straightforward. And then the commercial profile, the commercial risk is somewhat lower than the restrict strategy because you can use these in the United States. Now, you can mix and match all of these strategies, right? And we have seen projects that have done, you know, Regulation S outside the United States, transfer-restricted inside the United States. And then you can use these in the United States.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: And then you can use Regulation S is an exemption for securities.

segments.speaker: Miles Jennings
segments.text: That's right. That's if you exclude the U.S. and basically only issue your tokens outside the U.S. And all of this is setting up a pathway to achieve greater decentralization. And again, what I would argue is that if you actually do these correctly, if you do the homework and you implement them carefully, you can mitigate the vast majority of securities laws risk applicable to tokens in the U.S.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: If you're still trying to find product market fit, you want to be able to control as much as you can. Having points means that you can move quick. It's easier to be centralized and to perform these experiments and move quick and not worry about what all the stakeholders think because your project is smaller, you're moving nimbly, and people are less likely to be damaged because of the risk. So I think that's a good question. I think that's a good question. It's hard for things to have value if they're not transferable. So you can keep experimenting. So think of non-transferability, think of points as a way to maximize your nimbleness, your ability to find product market fit, while minimizing the risks that token holders could face if it was fully transferable.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: A good frame for a lot of networks or decentralized protocols is as marketplaces. And those people, those participants, they have expectations of the market that they're participating in. So on a layer one blockchain, people are typically buying compute, they're buying storage, they're buying network capacity indirectly. They're trying to commit a transaction to the network and they pay up front for that. That's the thing that they're purchasing. And what they expect is that the transaction will complete as submitted. It will finalize, it will be locked into the system. And only exactly what they asked for, will happen, not some unexpected other side effect. On the supply side, there are machines in this network that are performing the work of verifying the network and storing the things and transmitting the data and performing all the tasks of the protocol. And what they expect is if they do their job properly, they will be paid. If they run the program, if they abide by the protocol, they will be paid in some manner. And of course, there's variations. There's always sort of risks in the way that they're doing it. And so I think that's a good thing. These systems work like, you know, maybe it goes down or there's some congestion or something, depending on what type of system we're talking about. But the point is that the marketplace works by design to try to limit those risks and to make the process of buying or selling as direct and simple and predictable and guaranteeable as possible. That's the purpose of the marketplace. If you take this general frame, every network token is trying to do the same thing. If you think about the network token, it's trying to do the same thing. If you think about the protocol that you're trying to design, you are probably trying to design something that could be seen as a marketplace where there are expectations of quality or security from the supply side and from the demand side. And the role of your token is to do the work of ensuring that this is consistently provided by that protocol at all times with best effort and that the right stakeholders, are constantly incentivized to ensure that that remains the case. That is the purpose of the token at its most raw, its most fundamental. So the way some DeFi protocols work for the supply side is I come and I put in my collateral and I can trust that my collateral remains secure and that no one can just steal it from me or destroy it or something. And that within acceptable boundaries, I will be able to withdraw it or collect interest rates yields based on the functioning of the protocol. On the demand side, I expect to be able to deposit collateral and then to be able to borrow and to only risk liquidation under known parameters. No one will take my money. The assets that I borrow are legit and things like that. In other words, the whole construction of the system, the purpose is to ensure that both sides of the market are getting what they expect to get. At very close to the optimal price all the time and to make it very easy to reason about and very easy to build on. That is its whole purpose. So the point is that thinking of your protocol as a marketplace will make it clear what types of things your token should be paying for and subsidizing and also where your token can take fees to sustain its price, to preserve its value and to preserve its ability to keep performing this task. For the foreseeable future.

title: Governance Attack!?

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Hi, everyone. Welcome to this week's episode of Web3 with A6&C. This is Sonal, and today we're covering a recent so-called governance attack with Compound, as well as a broader topic of DAO governance, voting, and treasury attacks, how to avoid them, how to prevent them, even how to respond to them, and much more. By the way, DAO stands for Decentralized Autonomous Organizations, and they're important since decentralization is a key benefit of crypto and blockchains. Check out our show notes for links and other resources mentioned in this episode. As a reminder, none of the following should be taken as business, investment, legal, or tax advice. Please see a6andc.com slash disclosures for more important information, including a link to a list of our investments. Anyway, what happens when, in a system of governance, and this is also very common in political systems around the world, when you have activity from actors that the majority doesn't necessarily agree with? How do you distinguish between good faith and bad faith voting activity, especially on-chain, and other such tricky questions? To discuss all this, our expert guests are a6andc Crypto's Chief Technology Officer, Eddie Lazaran, our Head of Network Ops, Ross Schuhl, and a6andc Crypto Research Collaborator and Stanford Professor of Political Science, Andrew Hall, or Andy. We spend the first few minutes quickly recapping the exact sequence of recent Compound governance attack event a few weeks ago, including discussing whether governance attack is a right label for it or not, how it's different from other attacks, and the broader trend of online version of governance. We'll be right back. Ross, can you do a recap of all the events? Go ahead and kind of walk me through the play-by-play. What happened?

segments.speaker: Ross Schuhl
segments.text: Okay, so starting back in May, a pseudo-anonymous persona, named Humpy, put forward a series of proposals on compound finance to transfer a material quantity of compound tokens to a multi-sig wallet controlled by an unknown group of people named the Golden Boys. So on May 6th, the first proposal, Proposal 247, was created by the Humpy persona. And this proposal would have sent 92,000 comps from the Dow Treasury to the multi-sig controlled by the Golden Boys. It was defeated by a vote of over 700,000 to 95,000. On July 15th, the second proposal, Proposal 279, was put forward again. That again was going to transfer 92,000 comps. This vote was defeated by just shy of 600,000 votes to 120,000 votes.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: So these are very similar proposals, or were they the exact same proposals?

segments.speaker: Ross Schuhl
segments.text: So Proposal 279, slightly, differed from the original proposal in that the multi-sig was allegedly constrained to a limited number of transaction types. This could be seen as a response to the criticism that the community had to the first proposal. And then on July 23rd, the third proposal, Proposal 298, was created. This proposal went live going into a weekend on July 25th, and then ultimately passed on July 28th. Initial voting on that proposal suggested, that it would fail. However, in the last hour, the persona named Humpy voted with an additional previously unrevealed number of wallets for a total of 682,000 votes, valued at roughly $31 million at the time, tipping the balance of the vote and causing Proposal 289 to actually pass with 682,000 votes to 633,000 votes.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Wow, got it. And then just high level then, remind me, what was the proposal? Stated purpose?

segments.speaker: Ross Schuhl
segments.text: So the stated purpose of the proposal was to create a staking rewards program in which the comp transferred to the Golden Boys multi-sig would be deposited into a program called Gold Comp, which would be fully managed by the Golden Boys and earn yield on the deposited comp for the Dow Treasury.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: And that would make Humpy the largest comp delegate, I think, right?

segments.speaker: Ross Schuhl
segments.text: Yeah, that's correct. After this proposal, if it had been executed, it would have moved 500,000 comp tokens over to the Golden Boys multi-sig. And this would have left the Humpy persona with a voting power of just shy of 1.2 million comp tokens.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: You might say, well, that sounds pretty mild. I don't know, giving a little bit of additional power, but the result would be a non-trivial amount of voting power. It's not everything that was in the Treasury. It wasn't like coming along and draining the Treasury or something, but it radically expanded Humpy's existing, voting power in such a way that the vast majority of delegates was concerned that it could give Humpy total control over compound governance.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: I don't even know what we call this event or incident.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Well, the reason why I resist a little bit calling it an attack, specifically because I think when people hear governance attack, what they think of is analogous to maybe a 51% attack in proof of work or something like that, where someone gets a, an epsilon. title: State of Crypto 2024: Behind the scenes on stablecoins, AI, builder energy, more

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Welcome to Web3 with A16Z. I'm Robert Hackett, and today we've got a special episode. We're taking you behind the scenes of our newly released annual State of Crypto report, A16Z Crypto's analysis of the latest data and trends that have defined the industry in 2024. This year's report features some brand new insights, from estimating the number of real crypto users globally, to understanding how much interest in crypto swing states may have ahead of the U.S. election. We also dig into infrastructure improvements to blockchains and key applications, including stablecoins, AI, and so-called dPIN. Be sure to visit a16zcrypto.com for all this and more, including a new Builder Energy Dashboard, which we'll discuss on today's show. Joining me to talk about the findings are our lead data scientists, and we'll be talking about how crypto can be used to build a new industry. and report author, Darren Matsuoka, and CTO Eddie Lazarin. The first voice you'll hear after mine is Darren's, then Eddie's. As always, none of the content should be taken as tax, business, legal, or investment advice. See a16z.com slash disclosures for more important information, including a link to a list of our investments. This is our third year doing this. We've put together three of these State of Crypto reports. Why do we do it?

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: I think we like to use this report as an opportunity each year to really take a step back, kind of look at the industry holistically, analyze all of the data that we have access to. We consult with various experts across the industry and ultimately bring it all together into a single perspective.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, I think that's right. And I don't want to get too lofty with this, but crypto as an industry was born in the social media age. And that means that information constantly being streamed in real time. So I like to see the State of Crypto report as a way to pause for a second and sort of appreciate what's happened in the stream. You know, when you're in the mix of it and things are getting blasted at you multiple times a day, it can be hard to get a sense of the cumulative progress, let alone to remember what was getting streamed to you at full speed a year or two years ago. I think the State of Crypto report is a chance for us to take a step out of the firehose and to take a look at what's actually transpired.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: And to that point, our goal is not to just say that the crypto industry is great and get that message out regardless of what we're seeing. We really try to be intellectually honest based on data and what we're seeing out there.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Another thing I'll say is that crypto has a very overdeveloped in-group language, right? Not just because of the memes and the jokes and the personalities, but also because of the technical complexity. All those things together create this really strong in-group language. And I think of our report as a way to try to mainstream some of those big things. We try to avoid in-group language and make things very accessible through charts, through very simple explanations. And it's an opportunity to do that too.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: Also, it's a way to shift the way people talk about crypto because crypto is just fantastic. Filled with really difficult concepts and terms and to be able to kind of peel back the layers and talk about what is actually happening in a way that people can understand is a unique challenge.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Let's talk about how the report opens. So we're trying to summarize the state of crypto in the past year. So first up is size in the market. Because if we're talking about crypto, the first question you might have is, what are we talking about here? How big is the industry? How big is the market? And so that's the first question that we tackle here. Darren, you want to hit us off?

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: Sure. For many years in the past, we've been mostly focused on building the infrastructure to make all of this work. And so there really wasn't that big of a market for users of these crypto-based applications. But I think in 2024, we really did hit a tipping point with infrastructure improvements. These blockchains are becoming more and more accessible. They're cheaper to use. And as a result, we're starting to see a lot of people who are more familiar with the market and the market itself. And so that's the first question that we tackle here. to see applications come online. And now that we have these applications, I think it's really starting to come to life. And I think in the years to come, this will be an increasingly important question.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, I think that's right. Because beforehand, the technology was still coming together. And in many cases, it is still coming together. But we're actually beginning to see some applications. And that's something that if you haven't been paying attention to crypto, you might not realize.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: Totally. And I think the results speak for themselves, right? Active addresses. And I think that's a really important question. And I think that's a really important question. And I think that's a really important question. And I think that's a really important blockchains that we track hit an all-time high this last month of 220 million mobile wallet users. So data that we get from mobile app stores that also recently hit an all-time high of 27 million. And so, you know, the numbers do kind of speak to this idea that we are now seeing some of these breakout applications that are bringing real on-chain transactions and usage.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Let's go through each of those. You mentioned that active addresses are at an all-time high. Now, this is a kind of controversial metric. Not everybody loves talking about active addresses. One of the things they have going for them is that they are easy to measure. It's something that you can very easily quantify.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: I think to really understand why active addresses has become such a hot topic recently, I think we need to look at some of the historical context. I think in traditional software, most people tend to understand the basic concept of a user. Of course, there's ways to measure the quality of the user. There's a whole field of growth analytics around this topic. But in its most simple form, I think people can grok the idea of a daily active user or a monthly active user. It's just the number of people that are using your product and service on a daily or monthly basis. It's fairly straightforward. But in crypto, things are a lot trickier. And the reason is because on blockchains, user identities are pseudonymous. This means that it's very easy for one person to create and control a group of public addresses or identities. This is called a Sybil. And up until recently, a lot of the most popular blockchains had very limited capacity, which resulted in high transaction fees, which created this natural barrier for spinning up and using thousands or hundreds of different addresses. Because it would complicate the process. Because it would complicate the process. Because it would complicate the process. Because it would complicate the process. And it would come with significant monetary costs. But recently, we've seen that crypto infrastructure has become more scalable via L2s and new high throughput layer 1s, which has reduced the cost of transactions on many of these blockchains to nearly zero. And maybe you might ask, isn't it also near zero cost to create multiple identities on traditional internet applications? And I'd say yes, that's true for the most part. You know, I can create multiple email addresses. You can create multiple email addresses pretty easily. But the key difference here is that in the crypto world, we have very strong incentives for this type of behavior.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, Google doesn't give you money for opening a new Gmail account, unfortunately.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: Exactly. And a lot of new tokens today actually bootstrap their circulating supply by doing something called an airdrop, where you're rewarding retroactively users of the protocol based on a predefined set of addresses. And so as long as people believe that these tokens could be, there is a strong incentive for them to try to game the system by creating and transacting under many different identities. This is commonly known as airdrop farming.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, there's no reason that one person couldn't spin up hundreds of millions of addresses and just go wild engaging in totally useless and inauthentic activity in order to try to maximize their likelihood of extracting as many rewards as possible. And what we've seen over the last four or five years, that we've had airdrops and yield farms and so on, there's been a profound professionalization of the efforts to pull out those rewards. People have gotten really, really, really good. And I want to make it really clear to anybody who thinks that it is a gigabrain take to say monthly active addresses is simulable. Yes, we know that. And if you have a better way to measure using public data, you know, that's verifiable by everyone or that is easy to trust, I'd love to hear it. Tell me a better way. I'm thirsty for it. Tell me. Let's work together and let's figure it out because the space deserves really high quality transparent metrics. And that's what we're trying to do.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah. And so given what we know about these behaviors, how many users are there, right? Is it 10 million? Is it 50 million? Is it 100 million? This is the question that we've put a lot of work in and we're going to publish. Some of our thoughts around estimating that. And we'll include that blog post in the show notes, but maybe you could walk us through how you think about actually getting from this big number, this 220 million number. If that's the number of addresses, how do we get down to humans? How do we reduce that to the number of users, the actual flesh and bones on the other end of the screen?

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: I think one approach sounds kind of obvious, which is, you know, we've got these 220 million monthly active addresses. What if we could filter out? What we suspect are bots and symbols. And the way that we can do this is some techniques using on-chain analytics and forensics. One method is to filter out addresses that received funds from something called a dispersion contract. This is a smart contract whose sole purpose is to take in funds and automatically distribute them across many different addresses. Of course, there could be false positives here, but the activity, implies that the destination addresses all received funds from a single source, and therefore they're connected in some way. So there's certain types of filtering you can do around dispersion contracts specifically that can eliminate a lot of the bots that you might see. The other approach involves looking at addresses that have a near zero balance at both the beginning and end of the period that you're aggregating over. For example, if you're trying to find the address, to find the real number of monthly active users in September, you can look at addresses that had a zero balance on both September 1st and September 30th. This criteria implies that the addresses were transient in nature. I think bots and symbols typically want to clean up their balances after taking actions on chain, whereas real human users usually like to keep some balance in their wallets to cover future transaction fees.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: So this is like an address popped up, and then within like some period of time, people dumped into another thing and the address never was revived after that.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: Exactly. This is the type of behavior where it received a source of funds, it took some action, and then it pushed all of those funds outside. So it's a very transient type of activity that's often associated with bots and symbols. The other thing that we've looked into is just like the frequency of transactions. And so you can analyze these distributions to look at, did the address transact one time, two times, three times, four times, five or more times during the period. Addresses that have just one or two transactions, you know, at best, they're a low quality user. At worst, they're a bot or a Sybil. And especially over longer periods of time, this type of analysis can help you in some of that filtering. You can also specifically look at addresses that transacted very, very frequently over very short periods of time. Humans can only reasonably, through a wallet or app interface, process a certain number of transactions, whereas bots can do things much faster. And so you can do some filtering there. And then on the flip side, you can optimistically include addresses that are tied to some sort of identity protocol that requires some sort of setup cost. For instance, addresses that are tied to an ENS name or a Farcaster ID or some other linked social identity off-chain, those are likely to be real human users. So obviously it's very complicated, requires a lot of on-chain work, a lot of on-chain data and analytics and forensics. But we've done an internal analysis taking this approach, and I think we've come up with some decent estimates.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Okay, so the first method is basically you're starting with the number of active addresses, and you're trying to whittle it down based on culling the wheat from the chaff, culling out the addresses that seem transient, bot-like, or low quality.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: Yeah, so this is reducing the ceiling. We know what the ceiling is if there's 220 million. If there's 220 million monthly active addresses, then we're trying to lower the ceiling. And then we can use other methods to try to get at a reasonable estimate of the floor.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Okay, so let's walk through method number two then.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: So method number two says rather than looking at active addresses, let's look at some other sources of data that we might have access to, particularly off-chain data. And I think an obvious place to start here is wallet users. For example, in February, MetaMask, which is a popular Ethereum wallet, reported that they had 30% of their users, reported that they had 30 million monthly active users. And specifically, they define an active user as someone who either loads a page within the MetaMask extension or opens the mobile app at least once during any 30-day period. And assuming that we're looking to estimate transacting users, we need to make some sort of assumption on what percentage of MetaMask users actually end up transacting. And that requires some research. I don't think we're going to find the perfect number. We do have some sources that we can use. We can cite that say that 30% of users of MetaMask, at least on a daily basis, actually end up transacting. And so let's say 30 to 50 million. You can use some sort of assumption there. And based on that, you have 10 to 15 million users that we believe are transacting through the MetaMask wallet products. And then all we need to do is try to understand what MetaMask's market share in the wallet space is. Of course, the exact data here is not going to be readily available, but we can make some educated guesses based on what we know. For example, we do get pretty good estimates of MetaMask's market share on the mobile wallet side based on some data that we get from Sensor Tower. And so once we are able to estimate what MetaMask's market share is, we can simply extrapolate an estimate for the total number of crypto users from that 10 to 15 million that we discussed earlier, and then compare the results to approach number one, the filtering from active addresses to sort of triangulate on at least a range that we feel is in the right ballpark. And by the way, it doesn't just have to be MetaMask. We can also run this exact analysis for other wallet providers who have either reported their numbers publicly or are willing to share their proprietary data. And then, as I mentioned, just kind of triangulate on a range based on all of the information that we have. So we have done this. We have done this internal analysis. It is definitely not perfect. I'm not claiming at all that we are super, super scientific about this, but we feel comfortable saying that we think there are 30 to 60 million real monthly transacting crypto users today. Of course, this is still a small percentage of the monthly active addresses. It's 14 to 27% and an even smaller percentage, actually, of the total number of people who own the wallet. So this is a lot of people who own crypto but do not necessarily transact with crypto. It's actually only 5 to 10% of the 617 million reported by crypto.com, which means there's a huge opportunity here, right, to convert existing crypto owners into active crypto users by bringing them on chain.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: So the point is to say is not that, well, the Sybil analysis is perfect and we can end up at a flawless classification of which addresses are real humans and not. The point here is to get sort of an order of magnitude range so that we can look at growth. That's the goal. The goal is to be able to look back retrospectively and say we're bigger than we were last year. We're bigger than we were the year before that and see approximately how things are developing.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: I also have this kind of like funny way of thinking about this, too, where I'm like, well, maybe we're entering this new internet world where actually AI and bots and these things that are far more complex, and these things that are farming right now for rewards and trying to snatch up incentives that certain projects are offering. Maybe we're entering an internet where like bots and AI are just infused everywhere. Like why? Why are humans the major metric?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, look, I don't want to get too sci-fi, but everything is many to many now. Everything in a networked world is many to many. By that I mean like, does one person have a credit card? No, you have many. Is it only used by one person? No, like there are many internet accounts, cars, just everything is totally intertwined and entangled. And as we add AI agents and other such things, it's going to get even crazier. In a way, what we really want is we want to measure the attentional capacity that things command, right? The total amount of like cognitive bandwidth that they are commanding from the environment. But that's a really like sci-fi way out there. Getting kind of kooky conversation to have, Robert.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Next year's state of crypto report? Can we measure cognitive bandwidth? All right. So if we're sizing up the market, when we're looking at actual users and trying to think about the people who are using, interacting with crypto on a monthly basis, actual humans, we're looking at around 30 to 60 million, which is, as you said, five to 10% of the people who own crypto globally. So that's a lot of people. And then we're looking at, you know, we're looking at like, you know, like the, you know, the, you know, the, you know, the, the, the, you know, the people who own crypto globally and also a fraction of the active addresses too. Why such a wide range? Why 30 to 60? Like can you not get any more precise about it?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: I think if anybody has a problem after they hear the methodology, they might complain that it's not wide enough, right? And maybe it should be wider. Maybe it's 20 to 70 or 20 to 80 or something. It's hard to say exactly, very open to that. We're not pretending that this is a totally flawless analysis. The reason for the range is just contained in Darren's reasoning. reasoning, right? Like we have to make some estimates. There's some things you have to kind of guess, like the ratio of monthly active users as reported by MetaMask to the proportion of them that actually do transact per month, thereby becoming an active address, right? Like those are just things that we have to estimate. And I think it helps even just to think in orders of magnitude, like what we're looking at here, the fact that active monthly users of crypto is about tens of millions and global owners of crypto is hundreds of millions. There's a gap, there's a gulf there, but we're beginning to see the sort of outline of the market that we're trying to size up here.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: That's right. One thing that will be apparent to anybody who looks at this report is that it's not just that active addresses are at an all-time high, it's that there are a few blockchains that are driving this. segments.text: Well, I think the obvious answer here is that the blockchains that have the most active users are the ones that it's cheapest to transact on, right? And so I would take all of the face value monthly active addresses by chain data with a big grain of salt, because there, of course, is lots of nuance associated with this. But with all that being said, I'm going to base and Solana were some of the top destinations for crypto users in 2024. I don't want to take away from the great growth and development that those ecosystems have seen this year.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah, base has the most active addresses of any Ethereum chain that we looked at, at 22 million, exceeding even Ethereum, which has 6 million. So clearly, they've struck a chord.

segments.text: Totally. I was also interested to see Bitcoin up there. Like, Bitcoin's got 11 million by our count.

segments.text: Totally. And, you know, to keep going on this point, like the cost to transact on Bitcoin is not near zero, right? And for the first time, we now have a lot of tools internally that allow us to normalize a lot of that data, aggregate it in a way that we can share some of that data externally so that people could see the bigger trends here across blockchains, geography, technologies, and categories. And we've done that. We've made actually a dashboard that we call the builder energy dashboard. It has some very cool interactive data visualizations around all this data. And you can check it out at builderenergy.a16zcrypto.com.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: One thing I'll point out is we were talking about blockchains that have the most active addresses. And it's interesting to me that it sort of lines up with where builders are interested in building on and what they are building on. We've seen more and more builders interested in Solana and actually building projects on Solana. That was the biggest jump in interest that any blockchain had seen between this year and last year. Base as well, super high up there. And even Bitcoin in slot number three had a really big bump in people who want to build on it.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: Yep. And maybe just to add one clarifying point here, the way that we source this data comes from a question that we ask founders and builders we ask them simply what blockchain ecosystems would you be interested in building on? So it does not mean that there are this many builders specifically deploying contracts or that there are a certain number of protocols on these blockchains. It simply just means kind of where generally speaking, are these builders interested in building on? And I'd say still today, Ethereum and all of the layer twos, including base and arbitrum and optimism and ZK sync and the others still capture a majority. of the builder mindshare. So I think those were the key takeaways from that data, but you can check it out yourself by looking at the dashboard.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Excellent. So I think that's it for this episode of Crypto Talk. I hope you enjoyed it. And if you did, make sure you hit that like button and subscribe to our channel. Candidates for the, office of the president are talking about it. segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah. So crypto has become political in the sense that the crypto industry believes that political engagements can help resolve some of its major challenges. And a big question is whether that has made it to the mainstream or not. Have the crypto industry's issues become something that some voters or enough voters care about?

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Probably for me, the most interesting slide in the policy section of this report is this analysis of the crypto interest that we're seeing within various states of the union. Walk us through this analysis that you did here and what you found.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: I think everybody knows that this presidential election is shaping up to be a very tight race. I think it will. It will likely be decided by a handful of counties in a handful of swing states. And so, you know, we wanted to at least understand is the interest based on some of the data that we have, is it growing or shrinking across these different states? And so we basically used Google Trends data. We looked at a basket of search terms that were relevant to crypto. So we looked at Bitcoin, Ethereum, and crypto. And we looked specifically at the relative change in search terms. And we looked at the relative change in search terms. And we looked specifically at the relative change in search terms. And we looked specifically at the relative change in search interest as defined by Google Trends from 2020 to 2024. And we looked at which ones increased in rank and which states decreased in rank. And then we basically plotted that on a map and showed which ones had the highest growth, which ones had the highest declines, and then basically plotted all of that data. And I think if we look at the swing states specifically, I think the most interesting takeaway is that Pennsylvania and Wisconsin actually had top five, I think, of the swing states. And so we looked at the swing states and we looked at the swing states and we looked at the swing states. And so we looked at the swing states and we looked at the fourth and fifth in the ranks by change in this relative crypto-related search interest since 2020. And these are some of the tightest races currently, as indicated by various prediction markets. And so I think there is many different factors at play, and you need to be careful in what you conclude from a very simple analysis like this alone. But I think there is some evidence to suggest that crypto is relevant here and could have an impact on the market.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah, I mean, it's not a sentiment analysis, so we don't know exactly what those people who are searching for these crypto terms actually think about crypto. But we do know that they are searching for crypto terms, which just kind of indicates general interest and is pretty interesting to note in and of itself. You mentioned Pennsylvania and Wisconsin in the top five. Michigan is, I believe, number eight, so that's in the top 10. And then there were some declines. Some states saw a little bit of a drop in interest. One of those is Nevada, a place that has the city of Las Vegas, which is often associated with gambling. So if there's less crypto interest in Nevada, I don't know that that's necessarily a bad thing. But it is really interesting to see that, yeah, this election could come down to a few counties in a few states. And these are places where there is increasing interest in crypto, especially since last election in 2020.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: And just to add clarifying detail, data is by default from Google Trends population adjusted. So they are representing this data as a percentage of the total searches in the region. We don't actually have visibility into the actual total numbers by state.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: That's right. And total numbers anyway would be sort of skewed just toward the states that have the biggest populations, which wouldn't be extremely informative. Another really big thing that happened this year from a policy perspective was the Bitcoin. Ethereum exchange traded products were approved, and I'm deliberately calling them exchange traded products, ETPs and not ETFs, because ETFs, while they are a form of ETP, the underlying portfolios of them would have securities. Whereas in this case, these crypto ETPs were registered with the SEC in a way that indicates the underlying portfolios are not securities. Anyway, that's a really important point. But it is the first time we've ever had these market portfolios, these crypto ETPs, and we're seeing these kind of! Elon Musk Elon Macron have them, I think a tremendous tailwind for the industry.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: All right. Okay. Anyway, so let's dig into some of the other things we found in this report. People always ask this question, where are the crypto applications? But there's one that's kind of been staring people in the face for a long time, and that is stable coins.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Stable coins are a product that people are using a lot. It's a crypto product. I think people discount that. Well, stable coins have been in crypto for a long time. Supplies really started to pop off was that they were primarily being used as a means of settlement between centralized exchanges and also as collateral for DeFi protocols, something people could supply and lend in a protocol and then enable other people to borrow and create debts denominated in dollars, which is a lot easier to reason about than a debt denominated in Ethereum or some other volatile asset. Another reason why stable coins make a lot of sense as collateral is that if you're borrowing a fair amount on chain, it may be totally reasonable to pay a $30, $50, or even $100 transaction fee to perform that borrow because it still may be meaningfully more affordable than other ways to engage in an over-collateralized loan. And that is really the type of use case that we saw for people in 2021. In 2021 through 2023 period for stable coins. But as a result of the infrastructural improvements in 2024, where lots has come online, not just 4844, but the proliferation of L2s in Ethereum world generally.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: 4844 being the Denkun upgrade that Ethereum went through in March of this year, also called proto-dank sharding.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah. Now sending stable coins in a single transaction often costs a penny or less. So because of this, so just this year, because of these improvements, I think a lot of people are talking about stable coins as a medium for payment. Again, it's really interesting to me because I can't emphasize enough how, in 2022, this was just a non-starter. Everyone had given up on it. I don't think I heard the word payment in 2022, except to debunk the possibility that stable coins were being used for payments. Whereas now it's something that I'm hearing about all the time. And it's not just in a conventional, you know, pay for your coffee with crypto setting, although that is actually a real possibility. Coinbase had a pay for coffee with USDC day in New York not long ago. It was a lot of fun. But more concretely, now people are paying for their coffee with USDC. And so it's not just a non-starter. It's a non-starter. It's a non-starter. It's a non-starter. It's a non-starter. It's a non-starter. But more concretely, now people can play with blockchains as a piece of infrastructure to potentially bring down interchange fees and merchant network fees, all the types of fees that go into settling an actual consumer payment for something.

segments.text: I just pre-ordered a book from Stripe Press with the new Stripe Pay with Crypto feature. USDC on Solana was fantastic. It was just a really straightforward experience for me. segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Right. This is because you've got to still onboard to like a layer two network, say, an L2, a rollup, where the fees would be lower. title: Talking trends 2025 (part 2): AI x crypto

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Welcome to Web3 with A6&Z. I'm Sonal, and Robert and I are excited to bring you another of our two special end-of-year episodes, which also look ahead to 2025. Because we're covering our annual Big Ideas, which includes some tech trends individual team members are excited about, and you can check out the full list at a6andzcrypto.com slash bigideas. This episode is part two of two, but you don't have to listen to them in any particular order, covering the intersection of crypto and AI, from agents that have their own crypto wallets, and also AI agents in deep-in or decentralized physical infrastructure like energy grids, to proof of personhood and why having a unique ID matters in a world of deepfakes, bots, scams, and more, thanks to proliferating AI, and finally, moving beyond agents to decentralized, truly autonomous chatbots. Our guests for each of these are Cara Wu, Eddie Lazaran, and Daniel, a.k.a. Karma. But first, Robert. Robert and I began with some meta-commentary on the theme, and be sure to also check out our other Big Ideas episode, which covers other trends from stablecoins to app store distribution to infrastructure and more. As a reminder, none of the following is investment, business, legal, or tax advice. Please see a6andz.com slash disclosures for more important information, including a link to a list of our investments. Okay.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Okay, so what would you say are the interesting themes on the AI side?

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Yeah, AI and crypto was a big theme this year, and a bunch of people talked about it. And the thing that I really found intriguing about this set of big ideas is, okay, people talk about, like, artificial general intelligence, when are we going to get there, when is it going to qualify, you know, when is this stuff going to be smarter than people, when is the singularity going to happen? title: All about airdrops

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Welcome to Web3 with A16Z. I'm Robert Hackett. And today we're talking about one of the most familiar and most misunderstood mechanics in crypto, the airdrop. In this episode, we'll explore the history of airdrops in and outside crypto, the challenges of incentive design, and learnings from major airdrops to date. We'll also answer questions like how do you avoid civil attacks and professional airdrop farming? Should your drop be big or small? One time or ongoing? And what happens when AI agents enter the mix? To break it all down, we're joined by A16Z Crypto's Chief Technology Officer, Eddie Lazarin, A16Z Crypto data science partner, Darren Matsuoka, and A16Z Crypto research consultant, Andrew Hall, who is also a professor of political economy at Stanford's Graduate School of Business. Whether you're planning a token launch, looking for token rewards, or just curious why airdrops have become such a powerful mechanism in crypto, this is A16Z Crypto. This episode is for you. After my voice, you'll hear Eddie's, then Darren's, and finally Andy's. Let's dive in. So I thought we'd start maybe with the most obvious question, which is, what is an airdrop? Eddie, maybe you could kick us off.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: In very brief terms, an airdrop is when someone developing a token, they want to distribute it to people. And what they do is they hand out the token to a bunch of people in mass. It's like a large-scale distribution, typically according to some programmatic criteria. There's something quantifiable. And everybody receives some portion of token in exchange for either nothing or nominal participation in a protocol in some way. That's more or less the broad shape, but maybe in its most brief form, it's not. It's when a lot of people in a sudden moment receive a lot of token gifted to them from a protocol.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Why would a protocol, a project, why would they want to do this in the first place?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Well, there's actually a lot of reasons. One obvious reason is that when you distribute a lot of something of potential value, a lot of people pay attention. So it has marketing benefits. Another, maybe more substantive benefit, is if the token is related in some way to the control of an underlying protocol, then in that moment, you are also, to some degree, decentralizing that protocol because you are now distributing the actual control mechanism for it across a very wide group. There's retroactively incentivizing specific behaviors. There's forward incentivizing specific behaviors. Some people may believe that there will be additional airdrops. And in some cases, airdrops can be a continuous thing where people are being rewarded, for the continuous performance of some behavior.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: So if we're talking about, you know, somebody who might participate in an airdrop and receive an airdrop, the value seems kind of clear. You're being offered this idea of tokens. So you're kind of like giving responsibility to your users.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, in some cases, right, in cases where there is token governance of a protocol, you're very explicitly giving control over a component of the system that determines how that protocol functions going forward. In some cases where there's no token governance, maybe you're just giving them some portion of ownership in the network. So this is where we kind of get into the question, of what is the token for? In the case of a network token, it's possible that the token is integral to a specific decentralized network. In that case, it could have governance. It may not have governance. It may just have exposure to future fees or value captured by that network. Of course, if you just make a token that just has a name attached to it or a meme, and it's a meme coin, and you're distributing the meme coin broadly, well, it's kind of hard to say what's going on. I mean, you're distributing. You're giving ownership of some balance of a thing, and people may or may not speculate on it. Now, you might say, why would people distribute a meme coin? Well, a sort of underhanded example is where people will create a new meme coin that they hope others will speculate on, and they might distribute some of it in a surprise at its inception to other influential people, smart money, so to speak, people who are widely believed to be high-quality speculators, as part of an effort to legitimize, the distribution of the token. Look, this token is held by a lot of, like, you know, gigabrains. You should buy this token, right? And that's a little bit underhanded.

segments.text: Yeah, this is classic, like, just drop a whole bunch of coins on Vitalik, some, like, animal or pet-related coin.

segments.text: Yeah, exactly.
