That's, like, the other extreme. So the airdrop is one of those distribution methods. Of course, there's selling, there's earning. Airdropping is another one. And you can design them to be clever and achieve objectives of your project.

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: One thing I'll add that I think also gets overlooked sometimes in the broader, narrative is that the way the crypto industry works and, you know, largely due to airdrops and other types of incentives for the community, you end up in a situation where the project itself often gives up, you know, 50% of its total token supply to the community. A large part of that could come in the form of an airdrop. And this is radically different than how it works in the traditional equity markets. And I think it's important to remember that, like, for traditional startups, you know, you may end up in a situation, where you raise multiple rounds of funding from venture capital investors. Of course, you have your employee stock option pool that you use to incentivize, you know, early employees of the company. There's the, you know, founding team. And when the company goes public, you'll often see that investors own more than 50% of the equity. You know, the founders may have 20 to 30%. The employees may have the rest. But there is none that actually goes to the people who contributed to building that network. In the first place, right? In the case of Uber, the Uber drivers and riders got none of the equity in Uber, the company, whereas in crypto, it's become standard to effectively give up 40 to 50% of your token ownership to the community, which I think is a property that people take for granted and don't appreciate enough. Just how much crypto has changed the narrative when it comes to giving ownership to the community. And I think that's an important aspect of the world. of the crypto industry at large.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: It's worth pointing out, you know, some of these networks that you referenced like Uber and Airbnb, they did reserve some amount of stock options for early contributors to the network, people who were early hosts or who were, you know, early drivers. Andy, anything to add there?

segments.speaker: Andrew Hall
segments.text: The main things that I find interesting about airdrops is this ability in an early stage project to achieve a certain amount of money. And to attract the kinds of productive activity that you want in your network. So when network-based projects start, they tend to be multi-sided, like two-sided or more than two-sided. And a big part of spinning up a successful two-sided market of any kind is subsidizing one or both sides of that market to get activity going. And airdrops are a very interesting way to attempt to do that, both to give incentives by either explicitly or indirectly or implicitly saying there's going to be some sort of rewards for people who come and contribute, as well as by then giving those tokens out to potentially high-quality types, people who are going to continue to contribute in the future. The hope is it's a way to solve that sort of cold start problem and get the kind of activity that you want in your network. And then what's challenging sometimes is making sure that you're rewarding the right type of behavior. In cases where you're, say, a project has a very clearly defined notion of what value is that you can directly reward, then whether through an airdrop or some even more simple direct mechanical reward for that activity, you can get exactly what you need in the system. So if what you need is volume in your network transactions, that's what was valuable for your project, then that's something you can simply directly reward through an airdrop or otherwise, and you don't need to worry about it. You don't need to worry about whether the transaction is of a certain type or it can be mimicked or something like that because you're directly rewarding the thing that's of value. So if people want to come in and farm that thing, that's fine because they're doing the thing that is inherently valuable to your project. In many other circumstances, though, there's a gap between what constitutes high-quality activity in your network and what you're able to directly observe and reward. And a lot of what we've seen in airdrops has to do with trying to design them in ways to reward the thing that you can't quite measure and not fall into the trap of rewarding fake behavior.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: There's a lot in what Andy said there, and a few things I really want to underline, right? There are ways to solve that, of course, but they require ongoing complex work from specialists. You put USDC into the contract as collateral. Knowing what you deposited is about as easy a thing as is conceivable in a blockchain context. So it can reward people who deposited that in, for example, a DeFi lending protocol that needs to bootstrap its supply so there can be more borrowing and begin the bootstrapping of the protocol and its usage. So verifying supply is this critical thing. Another thing to zoom out a little bit is this framing of these protocols as marketplaces. We usually talk about the consistency of the marketplace and the consistency of the marketplace. But when we talk about continuous kinds of bootstrapping. But the airdrop is a funny thing because the airdrop can happen in a huge quantity instantly that not only incentivizes monetarily, but forward incentivizes the thing because now you're getting ownership in the underlying marketplace itself. They distribute OP tokens, which among other things are governance tokens. And they have both backwards and forward-looking properties. So Optimism in this case announces, you know, we're distributing this airdrop today. And there's sort of two parts to it. One is it is revealed who is getting rewarded and why the specific formula by which the rewards were determined. And so the first thing that does is give a new set of tokens to active participants in the network who can then use them in various ways. And then the second part is, you know, you can use them in various ways. And so the first thing that does is give a new set of tokens to active participants in the network who can then use them in various ways. And so the first thing that does is give a new set of tokens to active participants in the network who can then use them in various ways. So it empowers a set of hopefully good actors. That's the backward-looking part. And then the forward-looking part is that by announcing the formula, they are potentially providing information for the future about what types of behaviors they value that might get rewarded again in the future. And so what we looked at in the first project was there was an airdrop that was specifically about rewarding governance behavior, governance participation. And so we asked, if you've been participating in a project that's been rewarded, what are the types of behaviors that you've been able to use to get the most out of it? And so we asked, if you've been participating in governance and you received this airdrop, did it lead you to then participate more in the future and to deploy the tokens you just got for governance? Because a thing that people consider with airdrops is that the people who receive them may immediately sell them, which might be fine in terms of getting them into the hands of the people who most value them, but can have this sense of like, well, maybe we didn't successfully reward the right people if they didn't want to hold on to them. Again, it really depends on the token and the goals, whether it's a token or whether that's a good thing or a bad thing. But in this case, what we found was that people tended to hold on to these tokens and put them into the governance, into delegation, which was, I think, a stated goal.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: Was that surprising that most people didn't just try to offload them?

segments.speaker: Andrew Hall
segments.text: It definitely was top of mind for people at the time that other airdrops, including some of optimisms, have featured quite a lot of dumping, as people call it. So maybe it was surprising. On the other hand, the rewards were heavily tilted towards people who'd been quite actively participating in the governance, which you might think are not going to be people who want to exit. And then the second part was we looked at the effect of this revelation that, oh, we're going to be directly rewarding addresses who delegate their tokens. And what we see is in the first few days after the announcement that this is the reward function, we see a lot of new delegations into the system, presumably of addresses who want to hold on to the tokens. And so we looked at the effect of this revelation to see if we could get people to receive these types of rewards next time. So in that sense, it seems like the airdrop, at least to some extent, succeeded in both rewarding the right types of addresses, but maybe more importantly, creating this belief for the future. And I think that's a challenge. If you think about applying this to other projects, if that's the kind of thing you want to do, you have to be able to get this announcement out there so people see it and they know what's going to be rewarded. And there has to be this credibility where people think, you're going to follow up later and actually continue to reward the behavior you're saying you want to reward. Because if they don't believe that you're going to follow up and reward it in the future, then it's not going to create that jump up in the types of behaviors you're trying to incentivize.

segments.text: Of course, you might say, well, why not commit up front to rewarding specific types of behavior? In some cases, you can. segments.text: One thing I'd add to that is if these tokens are being used as a bootstrapping mechanism, then the time at which you want these tokens to be valuable is in the early days. segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: So the takeaway here is that it's in a lot of projects' interest to manage the volatility of their token. And one potential tool to do that is vesting and lockups. In this case, the goal was user retention. And we found surprisingly big effects on user retention, like getting bigger airdrop rewards led to higher user retention. And that I think is a playbook that could be used for other airdrops too, where if you have an internal DS person, or if you're able to work with someone outside on this, you can build in an analytics plan that helps you make your airdrops maximally effective going forward.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: On that point, is there anything to be said about how much of an impact it has on the airdrops? I think it's a really good question. I think it's a really good question. segments.text: And I think that's a really good point. I think it's a really good point. And I think it's a really good point. I think it's a really good point. segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin

segments.speaker: Daren Matsuoka
segments.text: Certainly, there will be frameworks and best practices that emerge as we get clearer about where things are going to be. And I think airdrops will be the same. segments.speaker: Andrew Hall
segments.text: The only thing I'll add is for the specific components of the airdrop problem we've talked about where there's this gap between what you can measure and reward and what you can't. An important class of those problems are ones in which the thing you can measure can always eventually be mimicked. That's a general problem that has been very well studied that's really interesting. It's called Batesian mimicry. It comes from evolutionary biology, where animals will evolve to be poisonous so that predators won't eat them. And it's actually not helpful to be poisonous to prevent a predator from eating you unless you have some visible marking unique to you. So the predator learns like, oh, don't eat the animal that has these markings because when I do eat that, I die. It's costly to produce a poison biologically. And so a species will separate itself by producing this poison and a unique set of markings to indicate that it is poisonous. Once that works and it succeeds, and that animal is now somewhat free from predation, what then always happens now is that it's not going to be poisonous. It's going to be poisonous. And so the predator is going to be toxic. And so the predator is going to be toxic. And so the predator is going to be another animal evolves that has the same exact markings, but no poison because producing the poison is costly. So you get to free ride by evolving to have the same outward appearance without the cost of actually having the poison. And then this eventually undoes the value of the markings because then the predators eventually learn that no, the markings no longer guarantee that this thing is toxic. And that has been deeply studied as a model. And what's remarkable is this is a model with no evidence. And so the predator learns that no, the markings no longer carry any evidence that there's any evidence that there's any evidence that there's any evidence that can't be mimicked, then there's no equilibrium. And I think there's a whole class of these problems we're talking about that are the same way, where if the only things you can measure to provide your award is based on can eventually always be mimicked, then it will have to always be a cat and mouse game where we want to be one step ahead of the mimicry.

segments.speaker: Robert Hackett
segments.text: I couldn't ask for a better closing statement. We can all go off and ponder which animals we can touch and which ones are going to kill us. All right. Thank you all.

segments.text: Thank you. Thanks, guys. It's fun. Good to see you all.

title: Your guide to tokens: Types, design, uses, more

segments.text: When people ran over to categories like meme coins or governance tokens that just like you're voting for like nothing, you don't even have direct technical control over the smart contracts through voting. In Ethereum, for example, Ethereum tokens are paid every block to block proposers and also to attesters, to basically the validators of the network who ensure that the network is continuing to progress, producing good blocks, all the computations are correct, and producing what we call block space, the high quality block space. And those validators get guaranteed payments. The Ethereum tokens that go to those validators are printed on every block. They don't collect Ethereum from transaction fees to pay for it. They just make Ethereum tokens and just pay the validators so that the network never stops and that it can basically fulfill its vision of being uninterrupted, permanent, 24-7, always functioning, always high quality, always secure. On the other side, because if you print tokens, forever, you could devalue them by expanding the supply to quickly add infinitum. title: Methods to the Madness

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Welcome to Web3 with A6NZ, a show about building the next generation of the internet from the team at A6NZ Crypto. That includes me, your host, Sonal Choksi, former showrunner and longtime host of the A6NZ Podcast Network, and now editor-in-chief at A6NZ Crypto. This new show is for understanding and going deeper on all things crypto and Web3, including by offering occasional data readouts and insights directly from the leading scientists and makers in the space. But in these initial episodes, we'll start by setting some quick context before we dive deep the rest of the season on topics ranging from auction design and mechanics, NFTs, securities, zero knowledge, gaming, decentralized media, tokenomics, history, infrastructure, roadmaps, and much, much, much more. In the form of everything from interviews to oral essays, and we'll always, as is a signature, respect your time and attention. So stay tuned. For today's episode, and to kick things off, we did an A6NZ Crypto hallway-style conversation on the markets, mental models for thinking about crypto, and briefly touching on methods and metrics, as well as sharing key trends that are top of mind. Our guests include Chris Dixon, founding general and managing partner at A6NZ Crypto, and Eddie Lazaran, head of engineering at A6NZ Crypto. Both were also co-authors, along with Darren Matsuoka, who led the data, and Robert Hackett, who led the editing. On our recently released State of Crypto report, which we refer to in this episode, and that you can find at a6nzcrypto.com slash state of crypto. For those that are interested, we also cover some FAQs and methodology that we didn't share in that report at the end of this episode after the credits, along with some behind the scenes. Finally, just a reminder that none of the following should be taken as business, legal, tax, or investment advice, nor be used to evaluate any security. Furthermore, companies mentioned are not subject to any financial or financial advice. are not representative of all A6NZ investments, nor are there any guarantees of their performance or future results. Please see a6nz.com slash disclosures for more important information, including a link to our investments. And with that, let's go.

If the work was done properly, there's no problem. After some period, those changes are accepted. But if there is a problem, someone can construct a proof and it's called a fraud proof. It's like a cryptographic object that shows definitively that something was calculated. Incorrectly. And if they post that fraud proof onto layer one, they actually get a reward for doing so. And then kind of in the nineties was, I think mostly dismissed as a kind of left-wing political movement. Almost every server you run, your Android phone is Linux. A lot of your iPhone is open source. So it's a very interesting story, which I don't think has been properly told. First of all, I think the main reason of one, it's a couple of things. One, it morphed from political movement into the technology movement and to fit strategically in with a lot of big corporate interests. So the biggest funder of Linux is Intel. Intel funds Linux as a counter. It's a counterweight to Windows and other proprietary operating systems that run on top of it. It's a complicated kind of strategy question. It's a wide. So that really works. But I think the most important thing is what we call composability. So composability is the idea that you can take a piece of software on GitHub or some other place and fork it, copy it and build another piece of software on top of it and assemble the software together the way you would Lego bricks. And this allows you, the community, the world to develop software in a much, much faster way.

That's one of the reasons that a lot of entrepreneurs and others don't understand what's going on in Web3 is that I'd like to describe it as an alternative planet where there's different laws of physics. And so things like all of these kind of lessons that we've learned over 10, 20 years on the traditional Internet are really flipped in Web3. So, for example. What I just described, this idea that you first go build a popular product and then you layer in kind of the community and the skin of the game and the monetization. That's flipped. The idea that in Web2, interoperability is a pain in the ass. Like, why would you interoperate? And so, like, for a long time and like 15 years ago, there were these things called mashups and APIs and other kinds of things, which basically all disappeared. There's almost effectively no consumer APIs anymore on the Internet. Like there's Stripe and business ones, but not consumer ones. They all went away. And they went away because interoperability turned out to be a pain in the ass. And a thing you don't want to do as a Web2 company, you just want to, like, build your network, put a wall around them, keep them in there and then, like, put ads in front of them, surveil them and put ads in front of them. That was the model. And in the Web3 model, it's no, you do want to interoperate because it's a way to acquire new users and to build your network and to grow your token and to have more people build on top of you and compose on top of you and build software on top of you. It's a very different way to think about things. You know, you said community. Community is many things. It's the users. It's developers. It's creative people. Like the way I think that Web3, it's the right way to build networks. We finally figured out after 30 years of the Internet and longer of building networks, the right way to build a network and the right way to incentivize network development. And once you do figure that out, it becomes all about how do you grow that network? How do you grow the developers? How do you grow the users? How do you grow the creators? How do you get the ecosystem going, people to build software around it and continue to grow it? So it becomes the only thing defensible, really, ultimately, because all the code and everything else is open source. Community is the only thing defensible in Web3.

A blob of short-lived data that can be used by transactions in the blockchain. What that means is that instead of properly storing in a fully robust manner, most data in Ethereum is stored more or less permanently. I'm oversimplifying a little bit. We may introduce some epochs, some valid lifespans of Ethereum data. But 4844 introduces a special area to store data in a special structure that's very cheap, very short-lived. Which means that a whole flurry of transactions can use that cheap and short-lived data store to do complicated things in a way that doesn't require persisting as much data in the long-term storage of the blockchain.

segments.text: Oh, yeah. Exactly right. Like the more that you store, the more expensive it is, the more onerous the requirements of the machines that validate and preserve this type of data. If you have a bunch that you want to do in a relatively short period and you really don't need to store all the data for it, but you do want to store, of course, the long-term consequences of all those transactions you're doing, then it sounds like a good idea to store in a temporary area, let a bunch of people use it, and then only persist what needs to be kept.

segments.text: Yeah, well, just building on that, in traditional programming, there have been all these trends over the last 20, 30 years. There have been these ebbs and flows. However, for smart contract programming, just because it's so new, we haven't had this type of history. In smart contract programming, which I think is very different, and I think move is some of the first evidence we've seen about how different it is and how differently you can design a language to accommodate that. I'd love to get into what are the types of things that we expect to change? Like what are the potential static versus dynamic typing type of topics? And then the other thing is, I think, the most important thing is that we're going to have to be able to do a lot of things in smart contract programming that maybe haven't yet emerged, just because there's really only been one language. There's really only been Solidity.

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: Yeah, absolutely. So my take on programming language design is that programming languages are problem solving tools. So you look at say, JavaScript, for example, like, we need some programmability beyond just static CSS. And we need a way to talk about moving around DOM elements and all the communicating with the back end and all these sorts of things. And in that environment, the thing that was the most important was dynamism. Web pages constantly evolving for many reasons, like you don't just want to be refetching or redoing everything after the fact. So both in the language and the frameworks built on top of it, like things like React, it's all about the dynamism. It's all about reactivity. It's all about just making things as flexible as possible. And so that's one example of a language trend and why it went that way. And then you mentioned the language. mentioned the language. And then you mentioned the language. And then you mentioned the language. You also see a movement in the other direction, like static type systems, safety, more predictability. And I think a lot of where that trend came from is also with programming the web, less client side, but more on the back end. We started off with a Ruby on Rails or Python or all these like very dynamic back end languages, even JavaScript itself to some extent. And then folks started off by building these very nimble startups that worked well because they could leverage the dynamism of these languages to build products quickly and to iterate quickly. PHP and then Hack at Facebook is another example. And then over time, as these matured from these small startups into much larger companies that discovered, hey, it's really difficult to maintain these code bases, to refactor these code bases, to keep things safe. And so you started seeing a lot more interest in static type systems, both in languages to be used for back end programming and also retrofitting type systems onto existing languages like they did with Hack at Facebook, like Stripe has done with the Sorbet type system. So when you want to transfer some tokens to someone, you're not sending them some tokens. Instead, you have to do all this low level, go to this contract and then change the address and change the balance held by your address and their address. And you manipulate and mutate that contract. It's a very kind of counterintuitive way to think in the EVM, in Solidity. And that's kind of one of the first things that stuck out to me about Move, is that it is a little bit more like the way you would expect all of this to work naively, hearing about it described in English. And that's an interesting mindset shift is just to take for granted that the words we use in English don't necessarily map onto the programming model. In blockchains, yeah, there's just a lot of, you're in a weird jungle space and things are all around you. And they're just not really arranged how you'd expect.

segments.text: Building on Noah's answer, I think that's also mine where it's definitely going to be a surprise for someone who's trying to iterate into relevance.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: So I think as a smart contract programmer, you have to have an adversarial mindset, which may be very unfamiliar, even if you're a product person, which may be very unfamiliar if you're coming from elsewhere. Just because of the deployment model, like you're not only thinking about the intended usage of your application, you also have to be thinking about the unintended usage. And that's why I think smart contracts are so important. Because in conventional programming, there's ways to hide yourself from this or limit it to a small API surface, or use a firewall to limit what an attacker can do or these sorts of things. But in smart contracts, the whole point is what you're doing can interact with code you never imagined and do so safely. And so you have to think about that, the happy case of that and the sad case of that. And that's definitely a mindset shift compared to conventional programming.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Is this like a line in the sand that you're drawing that you think ought to be top of mind when we talk about how to write smart contracts?

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: Yeah. Absolutely. And this is actually my absolute favorite thing to talk about. I am very opinionated about this, which is the thing that smart contract language designers should be thinking about is safety. And that's like the main focus, maybe even the only focus until we can lock down this problem. Like I think smart contract safety is an existential threat to broader crypto adoption. And the reason I say this is because you look at Ethereum and Solidity, the EVM and Solidity, which is the most popular platform today, and you look at the early programmers who write this code, these are extremely highly qualified folks, they really understand the underlying blockchains, they're really skilled. They're really security minded, they know what they're doing. I think they take the responsibility of writing code that manages millions of billions of dollars very seriously. But still, if you look at the track record of smart contract security anywhere, it's just really, really abysmal. And not to the fault of these folks, I think it's just an extremely hard problem. And I think the languages make it harder. So if you look at something like my favorite website, rekt.news, you look at the leaderboard or you look at like these 10 hacks that are in the value of tens of millions, 100 millions of dollars are super routine, like a sort of weekly or monthly occurrence, depending on the size. And, you know, I think that's a really good way to kind of get a sense of what's going on. And at the same time, the smart contract developer community is really small, like there's about 5000 full time EVM programmers, which is really, really tiny compared to conventional developer communities. So if you buy this thesis that the folks we have are sort of the most hardcore and the most security minded now, but the development practices and like the security track record is unsustainable, then I think you conclude that there's no way this space is going to grow without making the security problem even worse, which maybe means like, it's just not going to grow at all. Or like folks who are sitting on the side, like big financial institutions or companies who want to get into Web3, but are looking at do I have to hire a smart contract developer, I'm going to get a hack for tens of millions of dollars, they're nervous about this, that narrative is only going to get worse for them, they might just stay on the sidelines. So I think, as someone who's trying to design a smart contract language, safety has to be the first thing you're thinking about.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: What I mean by safety is that we're both trying to prevent the programmers from shooting themselves in the foot, but even more so trying to give them the right primitives for being able to defend themselves from attacks, because smart contracts are a setting where you're deploying your code next to an attacker who can directly call into your code, can directly link against it. Your code is open source, or at least the bytecode is open source. And so this is the most adversarial programming environment we've ever seen with the highest stakes for getting things wrong. So I think if you're talking about smart contract programming design, it needs to begin and end with safety. And once we've locked down the safety issue, we can think more about expressively things, but that's my somewhat boring, but I think very opinionated answer.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: What types of features make or break safety? What are examples of things that just embody that?

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: So I think the number one thing is encapsulation broadly, and then we'll talk about specific sub features that correspond to that. So I think the number one thing is encapsulation broadly, and that corresponds to where it's basically when you write your code, you don't know what an attacker is going to try to do when they call it or link against it. And so you need to be able to reason about it without being able to anticipate what a creative attacker will do. So like, you want a strong type system, not just at the source level, but I think at the bytecode level, so that the guarantees you get when you're writing your source code will continue to hold when you publish your code on the blockchain, and when other folks depend on it, and when they want to link against it. In move, one thing we look at with this, the feature that's popular elsewhere, but we think is inherently unsuitable for smart contract programming is dynamic dispatch. Now, in conventional programming, interfaces and dynamic dispatch, this is the key abstracted mechanism. This is ubiquitous, right? You define an interface, and then other folks override the interface with logic that does something different, and then you program against the interface and everything works out great. But we have this cheeky saying where it's like, in a world where code is law, interfaces are a crime.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Ooh, that is cheeky. Let's pause there for a minute. In a world where code is law... Interfaces are a crime. Can you explain that a little bit more before you keep going? I don't want to just skip over that.

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: Yeah, yeah, absolutely. So the idea here is like an interface, it doesn't define behavior. It defines intended behavior. Like when you read the specification for an interface or the parameter names or even the types, it tells you what you want to be done, but there's no guarantee that that's actually going to be the case. And so if you're writing some code that's supposed to be protecting something, but you're basically leaving a blank check for an attacker or someone else to fill in, and you know, this is contract law, that seems like a crime. It's an ill-specified contract. So, you know, big fan of this feature in conventional programming languages, of course, but we think in smart contracts, this has led to lots and lots of different issues. Like reentrancy requires dynamic dispatch, for example, you take wide dynamic dispatch, you just don't have reentrancy at all. Lots of things like poison tokens in Ethereum, like this happens because you override the transfer function, which everyone knows like intuitively, this is just supposed to move money around and maybe not do anything else. But now you make it do something super unexpected and steal money. And so that's one example of a feature where if you take it out, like reasoning about your code and having proper encapsulation just becomes much, much easier.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Because every time you see a call site, you know exactly what it's going to do. And you don't have to worry about someone else coming and doing something that surprises you later on.

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: Exactly. I think the analogy there is like, if you define an interface around a chair, four legs and a seat, you're describing it at a specific level, you're describing it not at a behavioral level, you're not describing other things that may be implicit or assumed about a chair, like it has a certain structural integrity, right? It has certain materials, like it's oriented a specific way. All of those things can be subverted and mean that the interface is really just an arbitrary set of descriptions that just doesn't capture the safety constraints that you want to enforce in that object. It's just defining things at the wrong level.

segments.speaker: Noah Citron
segments.text: I'm curious though, how do you then sort of do modularity really well without interfaces? Like how do you get that like super expressive plug and play modularity without sort of like standardized interface?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, how do you get inheritance? How do you like all these things that are kind of related to interfaces?

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: This is exactly the right question to ask. There's like, okay, you can rail against this all day, but then how do you still provide something that lets you write useful code? It's like, there's no crime if you're not allowed to come out of your house or something. So the way we do that, the interfaces and inheritance are not the only way. I tend to think in terms of composition. And so we provide several composition primitives that don't require dynamic dispatcher interfaces. So one is generics. We have runtime generics that look a lot like what you have in the CLR. So let me give a very concrete example because otherwise this gets abstract pretty quickly. Something like ERC-20. This is obviously a fundamentally important crypto standard. If you can't do something like that, your platform or your language is not going to be very useful. So the way that works in a language like move is you define a type called point of T, where T is a generic type parameter. And then you implement functions for coin. There's a function for joining two points together. This works the same for all coin types. It's not something that someone wants to override. You define splitting. And then when you want to let somebody customize behavior of coin, use these so-called capability patterns. For a coin, one thing you might want to customize is what's the total supply or more fundamentally, what's the policy for minting and burning it? So the way you express that is, okay, you have this struct coin of T, and then there's just different instantiations of T like USD or GDP or ER or whatever else. And then for stuff that you want to customize, you provide it at the capability level. The function for creating a coin, you say, here's a type parameter for my coin, and then it gives you back what's called a treasury capability. And then there's other logic that makes sure that only someone who holds the treasury capability of T can mint and burn coins of type T. And then you can go take that treasury capability. You lock in a different contract that enforces the total supply constraint. You put it somewhere that says anyone can mint it on Tuesday, but not on Thursdays. You go and lock it. You basically invert the control flow instead of putting it inside an interface. And then you go and lock it. You basically invert the control flow instead of putting it inside an interface. And then you lock it. You lock it. You basically invert the control flow instead of putting it inside an interface. And then that allows you to do this arbitrary sort of composition. And so that's one example of the trick, but this works basically everywhere where if you want behavior to work one way, you've hard-coded, it sounds bad, but you implement specific methods, then you define these capabilities for stuff that you want to allow.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: So you think of capabilities as constraints that you can add to what might look like an interface to a conventional programmer?

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: Exactly right. I think most of these interface patterns, you can very straightforwardly not quite compile them. That's a little too hard, but say, here's the equivalent capability pattern for this thing you want to do with interfaces. By the way, Noah, do you find that satisfying? I mean, feel free to disagree. It's good to have a little conflict.

segments.speaker: Noah Citron
segments.text: So I do find it relatively satisfying because I think if you can have modules or I guess structs that have capability inside them, and those structs can be fed into modules that have very predefined behaviors, you can end up getting the same exact behavior. segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: Exactly like that. And then these capabilities are an explicit representation of things that you're allowed to do, so that has a lot of other benefits. segments.text: So maybe I'm cutting ahead to the prover, but are these capabilities visible to the type system? And are they visible at the time of static analysis for the program? segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: Yeah, so let me give a short answer to the question and then provide some background. Yes, they're visible to the prover and they're part of the type system. This is something that leveraged very heavily, but let me talk a little bit about structs and the moveability. So structs are like a little bit of a distraction. So in move, you have struct types and struct is like another language. It can have fields. These can be fields of primitive types. These can be other structs. And then the thing that's interesting and maybe more different about move is these structs have abilities. If you're familiar with Rust, abilities are a little bit like marker traits. They declare built-in operations that you're allowed to do on the struct. And just as importantly, if you don't have the ability, you can't do those things. And so to start with an easy one, one ability that's very important for financial programs, you can use structs to do all these things. And then the other one that's really interesting for programming is the ability to copy. If you have something that's a coin type or an NFT or something in the computer, it's just bytes, but you don't want to allow someone to copy that thing willy-nilly. And so in move, if you don't say has copy for your struct, then you're not allowed to use our equivalent of mem copy, like they said, copy operations. So things like integers and strings, they have copy, but if you don't want your type to have copy, then you just don't give it that. And then there are other abilities like discarding. So this is called the dropability. So this is where you get into elsewhere. If you want to be able to just drop something on the floor, let it go out of scope, overwrite it, then you give it drop. But if you don't give a drop, then the only way to eliminate it is whatever policy the module that defined that type wanted. And so to give a concrete example, there is something like you want to keep a total supply for your coin. You just don't give your coin drop. And then the only way to get rid of your coin is some burn function in the module that declared it, which then updates the total supply. So these are the sort of tricks you can type that you can play. And then there are several other abilities related to where you can put something in global storage, whether it can go there or not. I won't get into those as much. I think they're less relevant to the question, but basically like building into the prover and abilities like the prover is aware of the type system. It knows how to leverage the type system to figure things out like, oh, I know if something doesn't have copy, I don't have to worry about it being copied. And so if I write a specification that says there's only one of this thing, the prover can leverage that to prove this property. And so it really works hand in hand on top of the guarantees that the type system is giving you.

segments.speaker: Noah Citron
segments.text: As soon as you said drop, I wanted to talk about my favorite. Can you just like talk about what the pattern is and if there's other similar really cool things. It's one of my favorite weird things that arises from move.

segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: It's definitely a weird thing. So yeah, this is very closely related to abilities. So normally in a programming language, you don't have a lot of control over when someone can eliminate your value. Like maybe you have a destructor that says this is what happens when your value goes away. But you know, sometimes there's weak guarantees around a destructor being called or you can't just say something like you're not allowed to delete my type. Now, like that sounds like a weird thing to be able to say, like, why wouldn't you be allowed to delete something? But it actually ends up being very powerful, particularly in the setting where there's no dynamic dispatch. So I'll start with an example of hot potato and then we can work more into the guts like a flash loan. If you're doing a flash loan on ETH, this is a standard that you override where basically you're exposed a callback where it's like, hey, flash loan contract, like you give me money, call my call back and then take the money back or something. And move the way you do that instead is someone goes to the flash loan contract and they say, hey, like give me 50 SWE, but then they also give you the so called hot potato object. And the hot potato object is a struct that has no abilities. It doesn't have a value. It's just a kind of a copy. So you can't copy it. It doesn't have any of the global storage ones. You can't just like stash it away in a contract somewhere and it doesn't have drops so you can't get rid of it. So the only way to get rid of it is to pass it back to the flash loan contract. And the code in the flash on contract that lets you pass it back forces you to pay back the flash loan. And if you don't, it's not the program will fail at runtime. It won't even type check. So it's leveraging this interesting ability to control the destruction of your values. And then as a programmer to put arbitrary invariance on when are you allowed to like what needs to be satisfied in order to just destroy this and then just sort of making these like API call patterns are very explicit. Like another very obvious one is like if you want to force someone to call lock after they call unlock, but want to let them do whatever they want in between, you can also do that.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Exactly. Yeah, when I first heard about the hot potato pattern reminded me of like mute Texas like interesting uses of locks where purely for the purposes of ergonomics, you take advantage of the type system in order to force almost business logic level requirements in the way that specific objects are used, which I think is really clever. That feels like such a modern such a high quality use of ergonomics in programming languages that really I don't think is super common in traditional programming languages even yet, but is so obviously valuable in a smart contract environment where there's even more logical requirements on what is possible, what is not possible when you have so much value and so much security at stake.

segments.text: So before we go into like any particular examples, can you give a quick high level overview of the sort of object system that SWE has? What are objects, who own them, how objects own other objects, how that pairs of like modules, sort of the bird's eye of how it all works?

segments.text: Yeah, totally. So this is about the dialect of move, specifically SWE move. And the basis of this thing is a different structure of global storage than the original dialect of move that we use. So the original move, the global storage scheme was very much like in the account-based model like Ethereum is using. And in SWE, we instead want to use the same structure. We want to make things object-centric with a high level of motivation of being able to express fine-grained access as precisely as possible. Like this helps you do a lot of things that we won't get into on this podcast, like parallelize transactions more efficiently, tell wallet users what a transaction is going to do before they sign it and things of this nature. So in SWE, the structure of the global storage is it's a map from object ID to object. And then objects are just move structs that have this ability called key. Key says, hey, I'm allowed to be a key. I'm allowed to basically appear in the global object pool. And then every object has to have one of these. And then the object has to be a key. And then the object has to be a key. And then the object has to be a key. And then the object has to be a key. And then you can write entry point functions for your modules, and then they can directly take objects as input. And the SWE runtime is able to, if you're writing a transaction, it has object IDs. segments.speaker: Sam Blackshear
segments.text: Yeah, I worked in goal directed static analysis. So normally, when you do static analysis, the way you sort of do it is you look at an entire program and you build a database of facts about the program. And then you try to find bugs by querying that fact database. And this works well, but it tends not to scale well for really large programs, because you're having to look at the entire program and sort of do everything. Whereas you might have a very pointed question that's about one particular part in the program. Like if you're running your analyzer and CI, for example, you really want to report bugs that happen on that pull request, and you don't care so much about what happens in the rest of the program. And so in my dissertation, I was looking at goal directed static analysis, where it's like, hey, I want the efficiency of the static analysis to scale, not with the size of the input program, but with the complexity of the particular property that I care about. So if there's really simple reasoning for why this thing is safe, or why there's a bug, I want that to be really fast. Where if it's really intricate reasoning that requires looking at everything, then that'll be correspondingly safe. So that's where I started. So I'm really excited to be correspondingly slow. This is a useful thing to do, because one of the big issues with static analysis is just really slow, especially when you run on like, giant multi-million line programs. So I was working on new theory in this subfield of static analysis called abstract interpretation to make abstract interpretation more goal directed and property directed for both better efficiency and better precision.

So I have no idea what will happen with the economy. I think it's going to be a very, very difficult time. I think it's going to be a very difficult time for me and potential recession, inflation, all those things, including prices of any tech assets, whether they be crypto or non-crypto. We just really try to keep our head down and focus on infrastructure, applications, technology, founders, working with our founders to make sure they have the resources they need to build what they want to build. And ultimately, they're the ones who build these great things. And given what I see there, I'm very excited over the next year of all the products that will launch. So that's what I'm keeping my eye on. And I think it's very exciting.

segments.speaker: HOST
segments.text: Chris, you've got this great conceit around this tug of war between two cultures within crypto.

segments.speaker: Chris Dixon
segments.text: In our world, there's at least two, maybe more, but two significant different movements slash motivations of people involved in this space. And our view, as we make very clear in this report and generally throughout the years, is that a blockchain is a new type of computer that has a particularly useful application of building new networks that have a bunch of positive features. So that's kind of the lens by which we view our investing is how can we further advance that mission and hopefully nudge the internet into a new era where we have networks that are owned and operated by communities. That are built through composability, where the bulk of the money flows to the edges, to the creators and software developers who build it. That's what excites us. So in our culture, the computer, the technology vision is primary. To the extent there are speculative markets, that's a byproduct as it is in real estate. And that's fine. I mean, obviously, there has to be rules around these things and there need to be strict enforcement and regulation. And obviously, it's not fine when there's bad behavior as there was with FTX. I do think it's important to call out this distinction because I think in the public imagination, these two have been conflated and that when you see politicians saying, you know, we should ban crypto, which is becoming kind of a meme now, I believe they're referring to the casino aspects of it. I think that if they understood the deeper technological vision, that they would be actually quite supportive of that. So that's why I think that two cultures distinction is important and I think not as widely understood as it should be.

title: Chain choices: or, how to decide what blockchain to build on

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Welcome to Web3 with A6NZ, a show about building the next generation of the internet from the team at A6NZ Crypto. That includes me, your host, Sonal Choksi. Today's episode is about a popular topic for many builders in Web3, as well as people coming into the space for the first time, or for just anyone interested in tech innovation and the evolution of infrastructure, which is deciding which blockchain to build on. It's a nuanced topic, so we dive into all the tangly threads here. The first third of the conversation focuses on the overall landscape, orienting us into the broader implications, why it matters, useful definitions, and a quick sketch of how the space is shaking out so far. And then, after exploring that terrain, we focus the rest of the episode on common questions, specific trade-offs, and how to decide between different chain choices, including discussing tech specs, community, marketing and ecosystem support, security, custody, programming languages, much more. Our expert guests today are A6NZ Crypto's Guy Wu.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Today, we're talking about a topic that's super top of mind, and it's a topic that's been for all kinds of founders and builders in the crypto space, and actually, even for people entering the space. And one of the questions that comes up is, actually, in any technology company, one of the fundamental questions you think about is, what is your tech stack going to be? And in the world of blockchains and crypto, that's actually a much more nuanced question. So before we actually start talking about the topic of this episode, which is chain choices, or how to think about choosing your blockchain, why does it matter? I mean, in other fields, you might just be like, oh, I can just switch, and we're so used to technology that is kind of fungible. Like, oh, I'm just going to switch my data service provider. I'm going to switch my so-and-so.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, if you wanted to switch from Google Cloud to AWS to DigitalOcean to whatever, it's a seemingly straightforward path to do that, and it's different for blockchains. Yeah.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: And so why is it harder here?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah. One obvious one is that if you're deploying a system that you hope to be decentralized and outside of your control, definitionally, you can't pack up everything and just move it. Right? Deploy a system that other people can build on and take its properties for granted. That's part of what makes crypto so exciting. If that's what's happened, then you can't sweep up everybody and move them. That makes it a little bit harder. Now, of course, everybody could coordinate and do a migration together, but it's a lot more work to do that. The second is that if you think of a blockchain computer as a shared state computer, a shared state machine, and if the applications you've developed are working, they're probably intertwined with all kinds of other applications that makes it harder to move because where you're going isn't where all the stuff you're already integrated with exists. So those technological properties make it harder.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Why it matters, partly because it's hard to move. Yeah.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Right. That's like a big part of it. But the choice of chain affects the gas fees. It affects the security model. It affects the tools you have available. It affects the ecosystem that you're meshing yourself with. There's a lot of consequences that come with this type of decision.

segments.speaker: Guy Wuollet
segments.text: Yeah. So it naturally comes up a lot as a top question. A few points here. I think it matters, one, because the switching costs are so high for all of the reasons Eddie just articulated. And I think there are two good examples of projects that have successfully switched. And even in those cases, it demonstrates why it's so challenging. The first being Helium, which initially launched its own blockchain and has recently decided to move over to Solana, a process that has taken quite a while. But it's a process that's been going on for a long time. And I think it's a great way to start a conversation about the future of Solana, a process that has taken quite a bit of time and effort, not only getting the community to help form consensus around that choice, but also just on the engineering side. There's also a game called Axie Infinity that originally started on Ethereum mainnet and then moved over to Loom, which was an early scaling solution, and finally decided to deploy its own blockchain called Ronin.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yep.

segments.speaker: Guy Wuollet
segments.text: And in both of those cases, I think it was easier to switch than for the average crypto application because they were both useful in their own context and didn't compose a lot of the money that was going to be spent on the blockchain. So it's a great way to with other projects. And so I think, one, it's hard to align the community. Two, it's a significant technical challenge. And then three, to the extent you have significant integrations and you've composed with other projects on a given chain, it's almost impossible to move over after some point.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Right. And this goes to what Eddie was saying about the ecosystem you're meshing with, but also just, again, for the listeners who might still be new to the space, when you say compose, you mean it in the context of composability, which is one of the key features we care so much about in crypto and blockchains, the ability to essentially use these elements. Like Lego blocks that people can build on top of each other, et cetera, et cetera.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah. You can think of this like if you've built a foundation of a house, it's hard to move the house without destroying that foundation, especially if people are building things on top of your protocol and those things built on top of your protocol are valuable and highly used, then you can't sort of smash that foundation down without destroying everything built on top.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Great. Let's actually set a little bit more context about what is a blockchain and then kind of the elements that make that up.

segments.speaker: Guy Wuollet
segments.text: Yeah. So when I refer to a blockchain or a chain in this context and choosing a chain, I'm thinking about what is the set of infrastructure that enables me as a developer, as a founder to build a decentralized application. And almost all the time, we're going to talk about smart contract platforms that have some notion of consensus, allow me to deploy an application on what you can imagine is sort of like a world computer. There are multiple components of this. You might hear things like L1 or L2, and you can decompose it into multiple layers of a technical stack. But we're probably going to refer to this as a chain or a blockchain in this context. And that's really what we mean. And when you're choosing a chain, there are a lot of different technical dimensions that we'll get into. But there are also dimensions around the user experience, around the community you're building, around the other applications on the same chain that you can interoperate with and compose with that you can put those Lego pieces together, essentially. And those are all considerations that you as someone who's building a new project and protocol should take into consideration.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: I'd also say maybe just for the builder, the way to think about what a chain is, is it's the place you deploy your application. So we're at a really interesting point in the development of blockchains. Only a few years ago, there were very few actual live choices. And right now there are tons of choices, and they're all scattered across the trade-off space. And exactly how those choices will play out is not yet clear. Many of the most interesting choices that people talk about aren't even fully formed yet, are just at the horizon. We're in this weird moment in time, this exact moment in time, where I think it is probably the hardest to make the decision. And it'll get a lot easier in the coming years. But if you're trying to take advantage of some of the new technology right now, you need to make a choice right now, at least provisionally.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: And underneath the hood, there are all kinds of possibilities in terms of, well, where's the data stored? What is the exact execution environment I'm running on? Where's the consensus being held? Whatever, all those types of things. But some of the options in the framework that we're going to talk through are going to just assume it's a place where you deploy your application and abstracting those properties away. Take Ethereum as an example. In Ethereum, the L1 today, execution and data availability and consensus are all happening in the same machine. So we typically think of that. It's like the blockchain bundle. But it is possible to separate them out to some extent in some very interesting ways. One is if you look at an L2 on Ethereum, that is a way to move execution to a different set of machines, but to keep data availability on Ethereum. There's even ways to separate components of consensus away from that. There's some so-called modular blockchains where they're considering really separating each of these components out. So there's some so-called modular blockchains where they're considering really separating each of these components out. But I still think that although it's very interesting to get into those details, they're only one part of the story in terms of a chain choice. And even then, there's probably only one place that you're really thinking about deploying your application. When you write the actual code, you're kind of pointing it at one thing. It's possible those other components have been bundled with it or separated from it.

segments.speaker: Guy Wuollet
segments.text: To make this very concrete, you can spend a lot of time trying to pick a different Ethereum L2 and then spend a lot of time thinking about which shared sequencer might I choose and which data availability solution might I use, Celestia, Ethereum, something like EigenDA. I tend to think about the blockchain design space as being highly dimensional. And this is something we talk about on our team a lot, as each blockchain being a vector in this multidimensional design space. I think this is a useful way to think about it, especially from an engineering standpoint. And there's a good reason so many people have tried to build different blockchains. There's a good reason why there are so many choices, because there are a lot of valid choices that you as a builder or developer wanting to deploy a smart contract might choose. And it's further useful to say that there aren't one or two trade-offs that are important, but there are a lot of different trade-offs. You almost need to be like doing our job or an academic to go through and really look at each of the different choices today. And it's not going to be the way, at least in my opinion, developers will pick where they deploy their applications. Like, it's not going to be it's not that complicated in the traditional world, and it can't be in crypto moving forward. And so the way I think about this is founders and builders should essentially stack rank the dimensions that they care about most and then try to find the vector in that highly dimensional design space that is closest aligned to what their ideal would be. And to me, that's really the process of choosing a blockchain.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: So that's what you mean by multidimensional?

segments.speaker: Guy Wuollet
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: So this is definitely a little bit oversimplified, but if we take two of the things which is data availability and execution, one of the most interesting ways that efforts to scale blockchains have separated is along that line. Like, do we separate out execution and data availability, or do we keep them together and try to scale with them together? That's one of the key kind of rifts.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Oh, yeah, yeah. If you think of that that way, there are layer ones, and that include new layer ones. We can talk about some of their properties. And there are this kind of amorphous category of almost application-based, application-specific, or sharded sort of roll-up blockchains, where there's one thing at the center, like Ethereum. Maybe there's not really a specific thing at the center, but kind of a protocol that connects the parts. And then there's these many, many, many different chains that are all interacting together, mediated or not by something in the middle. So on the one hand, there's new layer ones where all the core pieces are put together of data availability and execution. And then on the other hand, there are maybe these application-specific chains is one way to talk about it, where at the center, there may be a network that provides data availability or not, or a protocol to communicate about what data is available. And then at the periphery, there are a bunch of different chains that are all performing execution and coordinating like that. So very loosely speaking, those are the two categories that we're seeing emerge. Some examples of new layer, or of layer ones that are really pushing the envelope for scaling explicitly are like Solana, Sui, Aptos, Near. They're specifically trying to scale the L1s in and of themselves. And then these broader, maybe application-specific strategies, not necessarily application-specific, but where they separate execution are like Optimism, Arbitrum, Cosmos, ZkSync. Like they are thinking a lot about creating a separate execution area and then allowing scaling to bloom from there with maybe application-specific chains or things like that.

Anyway, little sidebar for a second. When you say the debate plays out at the same time, do you mean like the debate about the architectures kind of play out as the space is figuring out what to do with the application?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Exactly. In previous areas of computing, the constraints define the problem. And while that is absolutely true here, you can do so much more in software. It's kind of fascinating how the debate's playing out a little differently.

segments.text: I think that's right. segments.text: Yeah. Yeah. I mean, that's kind of weird.

segments.text: Yes, exactly. Definitely with like CK proving stuff and ASICs is an exception for Bitcoin. If you're building a game or a consumer app, oftentimes the marketing support from a foundation or from an ecosystem can be really helpful and really meaningful. And that would be as important as some of the technical differentiators for many folks.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, that's great. So building on what Guy said, two exercises that I like to run that address two of the key. Challenges. One is very quick napkin math in terms of what the expected cost of the system is. Like just play out for a second. How much data is being stored? How many transactions per day or per month or per minute do you expect from the users? That will quickly narrow the set of possible deployment strategies. Because if your system is a system that's relatively infrequent, large dollar values are being transmitted. At a time, then maybe the difference in transaction fee is actually not a huge deal. But if your system is obviously a type of system that is very sensitive to congestion and to increased gas fees, that's just going to make only a few possibilities even available to you, right? That's a very quick one. People can be kind of surprised by doing this napkin math thing. Like, wow, I didn't realize like these three things are just not an option for me.

segments.speaker: Guy Wuollet
segments.text: Yeah. The napkin math here. I would also encourage people not just to think about it. The average transaction throughput, but the peak transaction throughput, because that's when fees are going to be especially high. And that is in many cases when things are going to break.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Yeah. And it feels so obvious, the napkin math, but it's actually not like people probably don't do that.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: They really don't.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: They really don't. And like just a few specifics can really narrow it very fast. And it can also reveal opportunities for optimizational. Maybe certain things don't need to be here or whatever.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: I think that could sound intimidating to a builder. Like, oh, my God, does it mean I have to know everything? But no, you're just saying, like, figure out the goals. And then to a guy's point, you're only as strong as your weakest point, so to speak. But the third thing is I was even just thinking about this concretely for myself. Like, if my product were like a game where the goal is to trade NFTs and really transact around all these like micro objects on an hourly, daily, minute by minute basis versus another product where I'm doing something much more different behaviorally, then maybe I would make a very different choice based on that napkin math you just outlined. Like, I was just trying to think of a concept. If you have a simple application comparison for that, that might be super helpful here before you give the second heuristic.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Totally. It's very obvious that if you want to make a fully on-chain game, one of my favorite categories, that cannot be on L1 because every small game event is going to add up. Games may be constructed of thousands and thousands of different events. And if you're trying to save them all somewhere, you probably need upfront to think about where the game is going to be. Where that data is going to be stored, what actually needs to be stored. That's actually a great segue in my mind to the other exercise, which is to walk through step by step what you actually expect the user experience to look like tangibly. And that's starting at the boundaries of your application. Starting at like the boundaries of the user's needs.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: What do you mean by that?

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: First step, they need to get some ETH to an address from Coinbase. Like, that's not your application. But that may be where their journey needs to begin. In fact, it may even begin earlier. Like, they may have to have a choice of wallet. What wallet have they installed or have they purchased, right? All those different steps are totally different depending on an application. Some applications can say, no, first step is they show up on our website. And then we're going to integrate with the key management system. We have an on-ramp. We have gas subsidies, whatever, whatever, whatever. That means that the user journey actually starts here. If you go through each of those steps, you will see the things that you need or are missing. I just alluded to a few of them. Maybe you need a key management system. Maybe you want people to come with a wallet already. You expect them to already have a wallet set up and they're kind of onboarded. Maybe you'd like to hide the wallet from them. And you don't want them to think about the fact that they're using crypto. They just have an account. Maybe they can exit it. But you have like some non-custodial system for them to manage their own keys. That's kind of hidden for them behind like an email address or something. That can be a totally different type of thing. So I've presumed the existence of that type of key management system or wallet system. If you expect them to read and write from the blockchain many, many, many, many times, they need a nice RPC endpoint.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: Yeah, remote procedure call. Keep going.

segments.speaker: Eddy Lazzarin
segments.text: Yeah, they need a nice RPC endpoint. What do those look like? Maybe you need some analytics system. How do the analytics for applications developers look? If you're trying to show all kinds of numbers to users, is that going to be something you're going to build? Or is that something that has already been built? I'm just giving a few examples. But the point is, if you walk through the full actual expected user experience, you will see all the things that you need that likely have already been built or don't exist at all and are going to be your problem and so on. Those are all part of a bundle that you are inheriting by choosing a blockchain.

segments.speaker: Sonal Chokshi
segments.text: So I'm going to give you a name for those two heuristics, Eddie. The first one, let's call it Eddie's napkin rule, where you do the napkin math for like, here's what the... So the system is going to be. Okay, so now I want to probe on what you guys both mentioned about programming languages. You guys have said a number of things about programming.
